description.text:
This is Jämtland's most popular climbing area. The crag lies just a stone's throw from the village of Stugun.

The rock comprises several different rock types. It is mostly fine-grained granite, though some areas, such as Vita Väggen, consist of quartzite. Elsewhere, the rock resembles sandstone.

Stora Berget
This wall is about 80 m high, with an average angle of no more than 60–70 degrees. The rock texture varies, but is often uneven, with weathered knobs and rounded edges offering good friction. Face climbing on small edges therefore predominates on many routes, while pure friction climbing is less common than it first appears.

Protection is sometimes sparse, but generally adequate. There are exceptions, however, as noted in the route descriptions.

body.text:
This is Jämtland’s most popular climbing crag. It lies just a stone’s throw from the village of Stugun.

The crag consists of several different rock types. Most of it is fine-grained granite, but some areas, such as Vita Väggen, consist of quartzite. Elsewhere the rock resembles sandstone.

Stora Berget: This face is approximately 80 m high. Its average angle is no more than 60–70 degrees. The texture of the rock varies, but it is often uneven, with weathered knobs and rounded edges offering good friction. Face climbing on small edges therefore predominates on many routes, while pure friction climbing is less common than it first appears.

Protection is sometimes sparse, but generally adequate. There are exceptions, however, as noted in the route descriptions.

Take the Sollefteå road to Stugun.

Public transport: There are excellent bus services between Östersund and Stugun, followed by a one-kilometre walk to the crag. Alternatively, you can drive all the way. Turn left immediately after the bridge, then drive upstream along the river for about a kilometre and turn right at the Stuguberget sign. The path up to the crag begins to the left of the root cellar.

1. Stora berget 2. Plattan 3. Musväggen 4. Bräsväggen 5. Lilla berget (also known as fikahyllan) 6. Vita väggen 7. Film 2-väggen 8. Glömda väggen 9. Väggen i skogen 10. Gömda väggen 11. Shelter, toilet 12. Shelter, hut, toilet

From the top of Stora berget, walk towards the western descent. Follow the signs marked Plattan. The face is approximately 70 degrees and about 10 metres high. Use the trees at the top for belays and top ropes.

Musväggen is 10–25 m high and shaded by tall spruces. The climbing is fun and varied and, with a few isolated exceptions, not too difficult. It is a good crag for beginners and for warming up. Most routes are in the grade 5–6 range.
The face is in an open, south-facing position sheltered from the wind. A long climbing season is possible here, but visitors feel like popcorn on hot summer days.
In earlier guidebooks, this face is called fikahyllan.

This face forms part of Lilla berget. Its upper boundary includes Fikahyllan and the path leading up to it. The face is characterised by pale rock (quartzite) and relatively few prominent features. Face climbing, with some help from thin cracks, predominates. The rock becomes steeper in the lower right-hand section, where the hardest routes are also found.

Film 2-väggen lies approximately 60 m diagonally down and right from Lilla berget. The face is 12 m high and 20 m wide. Its name comes from an unknown ‘vandal’ having written ‘Film 2’ in large letters on the face.
The face is up to 50 m high at its highest point, and the rock is of good quality.

An old crag recently given a major overhaul, with new bolts, new routes and new anchors. Väggen i skogen has a north face and a south face. The topo below shows the south face. Its open position means that it dries quickly. The climbing is mainly face climbing on edges, though there is also some crack climbing. Fixed ropes have been installed both at the top and below the crag to make the approach easier.

Directions

From below: Walk up towards Frassehytta, turn right at the power line and follow it for approximately 150 m. A rope on the left-hand side of the face makes it easier to reach the ledge. If approaching from above, pass Film 2-väggen, turn right and you will soon reach the top of the crag.

South face

The matching of routes to their positions on the topo is uncertain, so be aware that there may be errors. Edit Media:Stugun_vaggen_i_skogen.svg if you wish to adjust the topo.

NOTE: Climbing is prohibited at Gömda Väggen during spring and summer until 15/7.

The rock closely resembles hard sandstone and is rich in cracks, setting it apart from the other crags at Stuguberget. It offers excellent, well-protected crack climbing, as well as good but poorly protected face climbing. The easiest approach is to head east from the campsite, down towards the residential area. Follow the upper road. Where the road turns downhill towards the village, head straight up through the woods towards the crag.

• Topos and route information taken from Erik Kuipers’ guidebook to Stugun (http://www.linkopingsklatterklubb.se/index.php/forare_sverige.html) (Autumn 2010)
• The information about sport climbing on this page is taken from Geir Söderin’s guidebook to sport climbing in Stugun (http://www.ostersundskk.se/forum/Stugun_sportklatterforare_20070911.pdf)
• The information about Stora Klippan comes from Fredrik Olofsson’s guidebook to Stora Klippan in Stugun (http://fredrikolofsson.se/forare/stugun_stora_klippan_2005-05-29.pdf) (ToDo: convert this to the wiki!)
• Östersunds Klätterklubb’s website, with more climbing in Jämtland (http://www.ostersundskk.se/klattring/stugun/stugun.htm)

• Guidebook from Coronn.com (http://www.coronn.com/TOPOS/sweden/stugun/stugun.html)

description.text: 1. 5 20m Climb the crack to the pine and juniper. 2. 6- 40m Climb the corner on the left. Avoid the roof by moving onto the arete on the left. Continue up the crack, over the next roof (crux), then follow the crack to the ledge. 3. 5 20m Climb to a roof, then move right into the crack. Follow it to the belay.
Climb a gully slightly to the left to reach the route. Then climb the slab towards a steeper wall, which is the crux. Bolt. Traverse slightly right, then continue towards the top.
From Laserdance towards the finish of Sorry. Uneven.
Corner and large crack to the right of Laserdance. Mixed?
Starts to the right of Jag visiterar, then goes straight up, trending right.
The route goes straight up the overhang to the left of Sorry, sharing the same anchor as Sorry.
Fine route to the left of Slem-Olas rute. Crux over a strange corner/roof.

Like several of the older routes, this route has almost been forgotten. Perhaps because of its rather unappealing name, or perhaps because of its hidden position far to the left on the wall. In any case, it offers fine and varied climbing and is well worth the slightly awkward approach. This is the only easy route between the large roofs on the left-hand part of the crag. It starts 15 m left of Åtlåten. 1. 5- 45 m Start up a ramp leading right. Climb over a small roof and into a fine crack. Follow the crack for a while, then move right into the corner. Continue up the corner and onto a ledge on the right. Carry on to the next ledge. Climb a ramp leftwards to a crack. Follow the crack until it ends, then continue up the wall to the pine. 2. Walk along the ledge to the chimney. Finish via Myrsten or Åtåten.

The 1st pitch offers superb, sustained climbing. The 2nd is more uneven but contains a very hard crux. This pitch can easily be bypassed. The route starts about 30 m left of Myrsten, in a large corner beneath the large roof. 1. 6 45m Start with an awkward layback up the large left-leaning corner. Move out beneath the roof, which is easier than it looks, and up into the crack. This continues, slightly thinner, up the wall to the ledge. 2. 7- 30m From the pine, climb straight up a thin corner. Follow it to a triangular roof. Climb over it and into the crack.
Very exposed route on the arete above Åtåten.

Fine wall climbing, finishing up a pillar. 1. 5+ 45m Wall and friction climbing. Friends and small wires are useful. 2. 5 30m Climb the pillar towards the top. 3. 5+ 10m Follow the crack all the way up.

Climb the thin crack to the right of Åtåten. Where the crack splits, follow the left-hand branch to Vänsterextremisten. Continue straight up the wall for a metre or so, then climb right and up towards the ledge. 1. 45m Protected with plenty of small wires. 2. 35m
Thin wall climbing with a roof crux.
Same start as Myrsten. Continue straight up where Myrsten traverses right on the 1st pitch.

This is where climbing on Stora Berget began: this route was the first complete ascent of the crag. That is probably its only merit, and accordingly it is now rarely, if ever, climbed. The 1st pitch offers reasonable climbing, but the rest is of a lower standard. It is therefore not recommended for the climbing itself, though you can always look for the cradle. The route starts at a huge flake leaning against the crag. 1. 4+ 30m From the top of the flake, climb to the left-hand corner. Avoid the roof by stepping onto the arete on the left. Layback on rounded holds along the left edge of the roof for a couple of metres to a small ledge. Traverse right onto the wall for a few metres, then climb straight up it (poorly protected) to a ledge. 2. 3 30m Go left across the ledge to the corner at its left-hand end. Climb the corner, then continue over ledges to a large pine. 3. 3 40m Continue up the chimney and on to the corner. Climb the corner for 20 m, then move right onto a ledge. 4. 3+ 20m From the right-hand end of the ledge, climb straight up, then move left to the corner. Continue up the corner to the ledge. 5. 3 40m Cross the ledge and climb the crack to the tip of the flake. Continue up the slab to the top. Instead of climbing this pitch, it is possible to scramble awkwardly up through the trees. Variation to pitches 3 and 4. 3a. 3 40m Climb the chimney. Continue right past the first corner. Go beneath the flake to the next corner and follow it for 20 m. 4a. 3 20m Go right and climb the two corners to the ledge.
From the first belay on Myrsten, traverse left along the crack to Slem-Olas rute.
1. 45m One bolt is slightly damaged by rockfall. 2. 35m
1. 5+ The pitch ends at an anchor consisting of two bolts (not shown). 2. 6/6+ Most of the pitch is grade 5+. A tree is used for protection at the top.
Fine wall climbing.

Start by climbing, for example, the first pitch of Mette mera to the belay. From the ledge, move left. Avoid the roof by climbing straight up from the pine. Then continue left with your feet on the lip of the roof. Exposed. Follow the thin crack, a short corner, then climb over the small overhang, which is the crux of the route. Sustained wall climbing then leads straight up to a corner with a fine crack. Climb the corner and pass the following roof on its left. Continue straight up towards a rounded corner. Climb the corner and continue towards the large ledge.
Starts after the first pitch of Mette mera.

Considered by many to be the finest route on Stora Berget. It is long, with sustained climbing throughout. The 1st pitch consists mostly of thin wall climbing that is not very steep but is quite hard to protect. The final 5 metres are steeper and include an unusual section just before the belay. The 2nd pitch is dominated by the route’s, and perhaps the entire crag’s, greatest attraction: a 40 m corner with an exceptionally consistent, fine crack. The route starts directly beneath the yellow patch on Öneväggen. 1. 5 40m First climb straight up for 2 m. Then traverse left onto the small ledge. Move right for a few metres and continue up a thin corner. Traverse left along a narrow ledge, then climb the crack leading towards the birch. Climb slightly left, then carefully straight up through the fractured section. Continue up the steep wall, finishing with a delicate mantle onto the ledge. 2. 5+ 45m Take a few steps right, then climb into the arched corner. Continue over the flakes to the left, towards the birch, and up the corner to the large ledge. There is a fine belay on the arete to the right, with a view of the entire 2nd pitch. A superb and exposed variation, Mette ännu mera, can be climbed at the start of the pitch. 3. 5 15m Cross the ledge and enter the corner. Continue up the corner to the top.
Climb straight up from the belay towards the corner, following the thin crack. Difficult balancey laybacking.

1. 6 25m The route starts directly below the left-hand edge of the long roof. Climb straight up via the flake towards the small corner and roof. Step out left through the roof. Crux. Continue up right into the low-angled corner and towards the bulbous corner at the right-hand edge of the roofs. Climb the corner. Excellent holds out on the right-hand edge. Then move left to reach the fine crack in the next roof. Follow the crack up and left. Step onto the next ledge and belay.

2. 5+ 40m Continue straight up the wall and into a corner. Follow the corner, then continue straight up to a bolt. Traverse a couple of metres left. Continue straight up for about twenty metres, then slightly right towards a thin, fine crack left of Jochen. Follow the crack to the next ledge.

3. 5- 15m Climb the short wall past the bolt. Finish either via the final pitch of Jochen, or by moving left along the ledge and climbing the finishing chimney of Mette Mera.

Originally climbed in 3 pitches, but climbing it in 2 pitches is recommended. The route is also known as Stefans led.

1. 6+ 45m The crux is the wall climbing after the anchor, which is not used as a belay. Belay on a small ledge (30x100 cm).

2. 35m
Start on the first pitch of Isabelle and branch left at the first bolt.

Warning: the route is run-out in places.

1. 6 50m Climb the wall towards a flake and a thin crack. Continue up the crack, then left across easier ground towards a curving, shallow corner. Climb the corner rightwards, then continue right towards a pale slab. Climb across the slab. Crux. Continue straight up into a small corner towards a pine.

2. 6- 40m From the pine, climb straight up towards the headwall and a small roof. Pass the roof on the left and continue upwards, trending left. Then climb back right and finish straight up the final steep section. Belay on the ledge. Either climb the short wall above the ledge or walk round to the left.

Excellent, well-protected climbing on the highest part of the crag.

1. 6- 48m Fine wall climbing, with the crux at the end.

2. 6+ 32m Easy at first up to the roof, which is the crux. A few delicate moves overcome it, then continue towards the top.

This route is rarely climbed nowadays. It is not one of the crag's main attractions. The climbing is awkward in places, with sections of looser rock. At one point it passes beneath loose blocks in an unpleasant position. On the plus side, perhaps, is the original line: a diagonal traverse from the foot to the top of the crag, making it the longest route on Stora Berget.

1. 4 30m The route starts at the same place as Spinning wheel. Traverse straight left along a couple of small ledges. Go round the bulge. Cross Ho chi minh leden and continue about 15m left along fine small ledges. Descend beneath a large block. Belay by a pine.

2. 5- 30m Continue left to a flake. Climb this to an overhang. Traverse left beneath the overhang, past a small birch, then slightly downwards beneath loose blocks towards a pine. Climb straight up via a thin, ‘suspect’ flake, then continue on somewhat loose holds to a leaning pine. Crux. Dubious protection in the thin flake. There are two variations to this pitch: Öneväggen and Omvägen.

3. 5- 30m From the pine, move left beneath the roofs—awkward in places—and up into a large crack.

4. 4 20m Continue left beneath the roofs and, with a little wriggling, up into the corner, then onto the ledge. Either scramble up between the trees or climb one more pitch.

5. 3 30m Move slightly left, climb into the crack and continue up the slab.
Climb the flake at the start of the second pitch of Önevägen. Then climb straight up and traverse on delicate footholds across the wall and over to the ledge.

Starts at the first belay of Önevägen.

1. 5+ 10m Climb straight up from the belay and move right beneath the roof. Being short helps here. Climb through the roof via a fine crack and up to a small ledge.

2. 6- 15m Continue across the small slab and down round the corner. Then follow Öneväggen across to the leaning pine.

The route starts slightly below and left of Spinning Wheel. The first 25–30 metres can also be used as an easier start to Spinning Wheel. If so, traverse right at the top of the corner at the start of the second pitch.

1. 5- 15m Traverse left across the wall to the corner. Follow the corner to the ledge and birch.

2. 5+ 40m Continue up to the corner. Where the corner ends, move left across the wall. Crux. A long way to the nearest runner. Continue to the next corner. Climb it for a few metres, then move left again towards the large pine.

3. 5 15m Move slightly left and up a small corner to the thin flake. Follow the flake and continue to the corner.

4. 5 40m Go round the corner to the left and continue traversing slightly downwards to the next corner. Follow it to a large ledge. An airy, fine belay can be made out right on the edge.

5. 5+ 10m Cross the ledge and choose either the large or the small crack to the top.

This is the true classic of Stuguberget. The name refers to the technique used on the harder slab sections during the first ascent. This route made the first complete ascent of the compact central section of Stora Berget. For several years it remained the test piece for elite climbers. Nowadays it rivals Röde Orm as the most frequently climbed route on Stora Berget. Well protected where necessary. There is, however, a slight question mark over the blade bolt, which is the only protection on a delicate passage near the end of the first pitch. The start is on a ledge directly below the highest point of the crag.

1. 5+ 45m Start by one of the pines on the ledge. Climb a flake formation for about 5m to a narrow ledge. Continue up the thin crack—the crux—for about 10m to a flake. Pass the flake on the right and continue to a ramp. Follow this diagonally up left to a ledge. From the right-hand edge of the ledge, climb the shallow corner to a bolt. Step onto the ledge on the right—slightly awkward—and follow it right until it widens. Belay at the upper left-hand corner of the ledge.

2. 5- 40m The pitch is mostly grade 3 climbing. The 5- grade applies to a short passage near the end of the pitch. Continue straight up the corner, or pass the flake on the left. Then follow the edge, move onto the right-hand wall and later onto a ledge to the left. From there, a short strenuous move leads onto the ledge on the right.

3. 5 30m From the belay, climb straight up onto the next ledge. Then traverse left. Place a nut as high as possible here to provide some protection for the following passage up the slab. Step farther left across an airy passage onto a ledge above a large overhang. Climb diagonally up right across the slab. Difficult to protect. Continue, trending slightly left, to a long narrow ledge, with dubious protection. Follow this left to the corner. Climb the corner and step up left.
From the second belay of Spinning Wheel, climb straight up and finish via a corner, which is the crux.

This excellent layback corner is a popular and equally worthwhile finishing variation to Spinning wheel and Ho chi minh leden. Climb the first pitch of Spinning wheel, then traverse left past a leaning pine and a block, descending beneath a thin flake called Bünnlüppen. Continue up onto the ledge and across to the corner. The traverse is 30m long and no harder than 5-. Ho chi minh leden shares its third belay with Jochen. Climb the corner for about 10m and step onto the ledge on the left. Continue up the wall for a few metres to the next large ledge. Step onto a flake at the right-hand edge of the ledge. Continue with about 15m of sparsely protected grade 4 climbing straight up the wall to the top.

A beautiful, airy route crossing two successive right-angled roofs. This route combines well with Spinning wheel. It is, however, slightly harder and more serious than Spinning wheel.

1. 4+ 15m From the top of the corner on Jochen, move right beneath the small roof to a fine pine.

2. 5+ 30m Climb the left-hand corner to the roof. Move out right and up into the next corner. Then move left beneath the roof and out onto the edge. Follow the crack, trending left. Watch for rope drag. Place protection in the crack at the outer edge of the roof lip.

3. 5 10m A slightly tricky crack leads towards the ledge, which provides a fine belay.

The route follows the arete to the right of Moster Olgas Utmaning. Start from the large ledge with several trees. Begin by climbing a tricky corner, then follow the right-hand wall of the arete all the way up.
Right of Spinning Wheel. 1. 6- 2. 6+

1. 6- 40m Start up a thin crack 10 m right of the start of Spinning Wheel. Where the crack ends, continue slightly left, then straight up on rounded holds and footholds towards the pine. 2. 4+ 25m Make a detour to the right, then continue straight up towards a bolt. 3. 6 25m Climb straight up the steep headwall.
Climb the first pitch of Ljunglöfs, then continue up left of Ett steg i taget.

A direct start to the final pitch of Två steg i taget. 1. 4+ 20m The route starts up a short, thin crack. Move right, then continue straight up to a ledge with a pine. Continue up a short wall to the next ledge. Belay. 2. 6 30m Continue up the next wall and into a corner leading to the large white ledge. Climb straight up the next corner, then move right on easier rock. Continue into the next corner, which leans left, then move slightly further left onto the face and straight up to the end of the roof and the final pitch of Två steg i taget. The pitch is poorly protected in places; run-out warning. Hanging belay beneath the roof.
Shares the start of Ljunglöfs as far as the belay ledge.

A superb direct start to Demokrati och valfrihet. The route offers fine face climbing and an excellent roof section. It starts a metre or so left of Tre i Ett. 1. Climb the face, trending left to the pale ledge. Continue a few metres to the bolt and belay there. 2. Continue straight up. Cross Två steg i taget and pass through the roof where it splits. Then continue up to Demokrati och valfrihet.

This route links three routes into a consistent and enjoyable climb all the way to the top. It starts directly below a large white patch in the middle of the face and also directly below the large roof. Start by following a crack for about 15 m. Where it reaches a ledge, continue left onto the face. Climb up via a few pockets to reach the next crack. Follow it to the ledge below the large corner. You are now on Två steg i taget. Climb the corner and continue straight up towards Märta Brodén. Then follow Märta Brodén to the top.
Traverse from the sloping roof on Märta Brodén, around the edge to the left and a few metres out onto the face. Then continue straight up.

An enjoyable alternative finish to Röde orm. The second pitch may look intimidating, but once the first 4–5 metres are over, good holds and nut placements keep appearing just where they are needed. 1. 3+ 20m From the start of the second pitch of Röde orm, traverse left across the face to a leaning tree. 2. 4 30m Face-climb straight up for a few metres, then continue up the corner to the top.

The first pitch is shared with Röde orm. The crux is at the start of the third pitch. 2. 5+ 40m Climb onto the ledge left of the belay. Continue diagonally left beneath the roof and out onto a large, long ledge. Climb the large corner on the right (slightly loose) to the large roof. Make an excellent traverse left beneath the roof. Hanging belay where the roof ends. 3. 6- 40m Continue straight up for 15 m, then trend slightly right towards the finishing corner. Follow the corner to the top.

This is perhaps the most frequently climbed route on Stora Berget. It offers consistently fine yet varied climbing, particularly on the second pitch. 1. 3 30m The start is the same as for Svarta tupp. Follow a thin ramp diagonally left for a few metres, then continue straight left along several ledges to a corner. Climb to a ledge, then move a couple of metres left to a crack with good holds and footholds. Climb this and continue to the pine. 2. 4 40m Climb diagonally left for a few metres to a ledge. Continue 2 m straight up to a small roof and traverse right beneath it on good undercuts. Continue upwards for about ten metres, trending right. Then follow the crack, which becomes a corner higher up. Belay on the ledge to the left. 3. 3 10m Follow the crack extending above the corner mentioned above to the top.
The leftmost bolt line on the headwall right of Röde Orm.

The route starts 20–30 m left of the large roofs, a few metres below a prominent corner. 1. 4 35m Climb to the corner and follow it to a ledge. Move 2 m right. Climb a roughly 5 m high, slightly overhanging section. (Crux, difficult to protect.) Follow the thin crack to a large pine. 2. 5+ 25m Easy climbing, trending slightly left to a ledge in an alcove. Step right onto the face and continue for 6 m on small but good footholds and holds along the thin crack. Then traverse right for about a metre before continuing straight up. Acceptable protection, mainly with wires.

Like Svarta Tupp, this features the face climbing on small but “well-formed” holds and footholds that is so typical of Stora Berget. Continue slightly further left of Svarta Tupp. Follow the thin crack to the top. Protection is widely spaced.

The lower section is difficult to protect and often wet. The start is 10 m left of the roof. Take the right-hand side of the gully, then continue diagonally right into the corner. Climb up 5 m, then move 3 m left and up into another corner. Continue over slabs to the large pine. Belay. Finish via the diagonal ramp (walk-off), or by another route, such as Röde orm or Svarta tupp.
Steep, fine face climbing on the pillar right of Svarta tupp. Nuts and bolts.
A finishing variation to Vått o torrt, straight over the roofs.

A knee layback is the key to this otherwise very “tricky” route. Difficult to protect but easy climbing up to the roof. Start as for Cirka nitti, but go straight over the roof instead of right. Then follow the corner all the way up.

Start in the corner below the roof. Traverse diagonally upwards to the left-hand edge of the roof. Move right between the roofs. Make an airy traverse for a few metres, then climb over the roof and continue towards a fine pine.

The crux has a tendency to produce “hairiness” where it is not normally found. Start at the large corner on the right below the large roof. Follow the corner to a large crack leading right. Hand-traverse the crack on good holds to the next corner. Climb the corner (crux), then move right onto the edge and up beneath the roof. To reach the poor crack, move slightly left and then straight up. After a few metres, trend left towards a beckoning pine. Belay. Continue upwards past bushes and over loose rock.
The slab left of Raka Ruben.

This is the finest and most consistent route up this section of wall. It starts by the large blocks. With a little planning and finesse, good protection can be found throughout. Start by climbing to a large flake. Follow it, then move left beneath the roof. Where it becomes a small corner, step right towards a large, prominent corner. Follow it. Move right at the roof and follow the crack to the pine. Belay. The best way down from the belay is to abseil.

The reason for the name soon becomes clear. The route starts in the obvious crack to the right of the start of Raka Ruben. Follow it to the roof. Traverse left beneath the roof to the corner of Raka Ruben. Climb this to the next roof. Traverse left to the next corner. Continue up this to a large horizontal crack. Follow it left, then hand-traverse down to the next corner. Downclimb this to the ledge and belay. You are now roughly level with the start.
Climb the obvious arete straight up towards the finishing crack of Velige Ville.
Roof and overhangs left of Samhällsparasiten.
Start as for Samhällsparasiten, but use the arete. Then move onto Ville direkt.
Wall climbing to the right of ALU. Do not use the arete.
The slab between Ville direkt and Velige Ville.

An interesting and varied route on steep rock, but without any major difficulties. Double ropes are useful. Start as for Smultronstället. Follow the thin cracks diagonally left. Traverse straight left for about 4m where it steepens. Pass the overhang (crux) to reach a good finger crack. Follow this to the pine. It is easiest to abseil from here, though it is also possible to continue upwards past some bushes and trees.
Follow this corner, painstakingly cleaned, all the way to a small finishing crux. The first section to the ledge is somewhat difficult to protect.

At the far right, where the wall is at its lowest, are several short, good cracks. The route follows the leftmost crack. First climb a short, shallow corner. Then move left through a delicate section to reach better holds in the crack. Follow the crack all the way to the top.
Climb the obvious corner at the far left of the wall. The slab can also be climbed.
Follow the two cracks to the right of the corner.

The route is actually a trad route, but there are now conveniently placed bolts. Start straight up on large holds and features. Move left after 6–7m. Continue up on small footholds to a ledge. There are two possible finishes from the ledge. 1. Follow the crack at the left edge of the ledge to a large block. Then either follow the larger crack straight over the block, or move right and follow the corner up. The left-hand corner can also be climbed. 2. Continue across the ledge and climb the narrow corner. Continue over a block and up the crack.
Starts directly beneath the thin but obvious crack. Follow the crack. Where it ends, round the corner to the left and continue left to a large crack. Follow the crack to the top.
Has an alternative overhanging finish to the left from the upper ledge.
The route starts 7–8m to the right of Mossiga musen and follows the corner all the way up.
Follow the crack up over the roof. (Easier if you brace a foot against the tree.) Move up right along the ramp, then climb the small corner to the ledge. Finish up the short crack.
There are some small roofs 10m to the right of Skogskalle. Start at the right-hand edge and surmount the roof with some difficulty. Continue up the crack.
The crack a few metres to the right of Startsvårigheter.
The pillar left of Skitiga stjärten.
Climb the chimney to the top of the block. Step onto the ledge and traverse left to a corner.

Start to the right of the chimney with easy climbing onto the ledge. Continue a metre or so up the wall. Traverse right to a thin crack. Then climb diagonally right up the ramp towards an obvious corner at the end of the route. Enter the corner and climb it.
Wall climbing starting in an obvious crack.
Alternative start to Autostrada.
1. From the ledge, climb the corner on the right. Continue traversing right to the ledge before Bräsvecket. 2. 5+ 10m Follow the crack left.
Thin, strenuous and cruxy, with few rests. Good – if you have the stamina.
Variant of Estrella.
A pronounced overhang on the outside of a corner.
Good wall climbing.
The pillar to the right of Lagerblad.
Wall to the right of Luta Lätt.
Crosses a small arete near the start.
Very thin wall climbing. The first bolted route left of the large corner in the middle of the wall.

The route starts directly beneath a large corner. Start in the small corner and follow it to the ledge. Begin by climbing the corner and the crack on the right-hand wall. Then move into the crack and follow it straight up to the ledge. It is slightly easier to take the right-hand crack. Continue up the right-hand edge of the wall above (which is somewhat difficult to protect).
Climb the large corner straight up. Move left beneath the roof, then climb the small corner.
The first line with expansion bolts to the right of the large corner in the middle of the wall. Start as for Örnnästet.
Crosses the roof just above the slab. Bolted by Ö. Söderin?

Starts with a short boulder problem on the small wall at the bottom, then trends right across the slab. Pass the roof on its right and enter a corner. Then step out onto the wall to a large hole and continue straight up.

Start up a corner, then continue up a flake to a ledge. Climb slightly left, then straight up into a roof-and-corner feature. Then power on up the very steep wall. Continue upwards from the last bolt. Reach far right for a good hold, then haul yourself onto the ledge. Go straight across the ledge and into a large diagonal crack in the roof. Climb into the crack, then move left onto the wall and continue straight up to the top.
Fun climbing and fairly steep for the grade.

The route follows several very thin cracks. Take the left-hand of the two cracks. The climbing to the ledge is good, thin and sustained. From the ledge, continue up the corner and over the roof on good jams. Then follow the crack to a small ledge and climb the short, slightly overhanging corner above.
The roofs far to the right, behind a large spruce.

The route starts from the large block in Kempeslukten. Move left from the block onto the ledge. At the end of the ledge, lower yourself down and hand-traverse the crack leftwards into the corner. Climb the corner for a few metres. Where it steepens, traverse left beneath the small roof, out onto the slab and up to the ledge. The same finish as Rückärmen can be used.
Do not traverse left out of the corner on Rückkragen. Instead, climb straight up the corner to the ledge. Cross the ledge and climb the next corner.
The route starts from the ledge 1m before the recess on Rückärmen. Climb the short wall to reach the crack, then follow it.

The thin but distinct crack on the overhanging wall left of Bagar Berta. Good climbing, with the crux near the top. There are no protection opportunities before the horizontal break. Ground-fall potential.
Runs between Fischerrisset and Randy rabbit. Good wall climbing and a strenuous move through the roof.
The chimney in the corner left of Bagar Berta.

A fairly good line up a prominent corner. The route is often wet. Walk out onto the block wedged in Kempeslukten. Step from the block into the corner and follow it to the top. The steep finish can be avoided by stepping onto a ledge to the left. The grade applies to this latter variation. Another variation follows the crack on the arete to the left.

The route starts from the ledge above Georgs grind. Make an exposed rising hand-traverse right along the crack and around the corner to a birch. From here it is easy to step up to the top, but you can also continue right for about 15 metres on a fairly easy traverse along a ledge of varying width.
Follow the projecting arete over the roofs to the ledge on Georgs grind. The crux is somewhat difficult to protect.
Climb the corner to the roof. Continue left beneath the roof and up onto a ledge, then climb the slab above.
Climb the corner and cross the roof via the thin crack. (First free ascent: R. Hedman, J. Liliemark)
Climb about 6m up the corner, then traverse right along a crack beneath the roof to Huva.

Initially follow the right-hand side of the pillar arete. The difficulties begin at the first roof with a delicate balance move on the left-hand side. Then move right. Awkward for tall climbers. A hard layback move finishes the route.
A direct start to Everest that makes the whole route more enjoyable. The crux is at the roof.
This route starts halfway up Huva. Step out left above the first roof. Traverse left beneath the second roof and climb through it. Climb the third roof trending left to reach the final crest.

Similar to the neighbouring Hickande giraffen in both angle and line, but an awkward crux near the finish spoils the comparison. Follow the crack past a small pine and a pointed block, then continue upwards.
Good slab climbing on small holds and footholds. Protected with nuts on the first ascent, but now fully bolted.

Consistently good climbing. 1. 4- 40m The route starts a metre or so left of the corner with the cracks. Climb the wall to a crack containing wedged blocks. Follow the crack past a block formation and continue up a small corner to a pine. 2. 3- 5m Continue to the top.
Start 1m right of Giraffen, then climb diagonally right towards the top.
Left of Hörnet på hörnet.
Climb over the small roof and up the corner. Somewhat difficult to protect.
Climb the small roof and the shallow corner immediately left of Stånkande Örnen.
The chimney is easier to climb but harder to protect the farther out you climb.
Climb the arete right of Stånkande örnen.

The short grade 5 section can easily be bypassed, reducing the overall difficulty to no more than grade 4. The lower section offers unusual and exposed laybacking and undercut climbing. 1. 5 25m Follow the crack and flake formation to the ledge. Continue up the corner (crux) to the next ledge. 2. 4- 25m Walk over to the large pine on the left, then continue right up a flake formation. Move a couple of metres left to the next flake and follow it for a metre or so. Then climb the crack splitting the flake and follow it to the top.
An alternative finish to Hängande hunden.
An alternative start to Hängande hunden. Climb up beneath the roof, then move left before heading straight up the thin crack.
The route climbs the slightly overhanging wall left of Jubileumsleden. Start from the top of the large block.
Start by climbing 1 metre of Diagonalen, then continue straight up the short, good jamming crack to the top.

The route starts in the groove left of the flake. Make an exposed rising hand-traverse right along the crack and around the corner to a birch. From here it is easy to step up to the top, but you can also continue right for about 15 metres on a fairly easy traverse along a ledge of varying width.
Bridge up the chimney to the tip of the block. Move right onto the wall and follow the shallow groove to Diagonalen. The crux is at the top.
The crux comes after about 5m. Small holds and good footwork solve the problem.

Start from the same place as Jubileumsleden. Climb diagonally right across the steep wall. From the second bolt, continue straight up and mantle onto a small ledge. Continue slightly right, then straight up, making a detour to the left before finally climbing to the top.
Starts 1 metre left of where Enok nitti crosses the roof. The first 4 metres are the crux: strenuous, overhanging climbing. The rest is easier slab climbing to the top.
Start up Hängda hunden, then move right above the roof and continue via the direct start of Cecilias insteg. Where the crack curves left, continue straight up the steep wall to Krälande krokodilen.
Climb the corner, then move left along a horizontal crack. After a metre or so, either go straight up (slightly difficult to protect) or continue out to Hängande hunden.

This route links Cecilias insteg with Sailors Delight. Start up Cecilias insteg and, after the corner, continue straight up the crack to a bolt. Move slightly right, then continue straight up towards the white patch on Sailors delight. A fine combination of two routes that avoids the crux of Sailors delight. With the start and finish mentioned above, the grade is 6-, b.

Climb to the obvious flake crack. Move right and continue up the crack as it becomes increasingly shallow. Pass the crux using tiny holds and footholds. Traverse left towards a white patch and a small ledge. Then climb over a small roof to a belay on a ledge.

Perhaps the finest line through the massive central section of Lilla berget. After only a couple of metres, shorter climbers receive a rude explanation of the route’s cryptic name.

1. 6- 20m Climb steeply to a decimetre-wide ledge. Move up using the crack on the right, then follow the crack into a corner. When the crack becomes too thin, move right onto Krälande krokodilen.

2. 6+ 20m Climb through the overhang on small holds. (A small nut can be placed at the crux while standing on the crocodile traverse.) Follow a narrow ledge right, just along the lip of the roof, then climb straight up a fine crack.

3. 3 10m Climb to the top.
Mixed if desired. Stugun’s ultimate test of nerve.

Start in a corner. Climb on good holds to an alcove 5 metres above the ground. (Good nut placement at the top of the alcove.) Climb through the alcove overhang (crux), then continue up a fine layback crack that becomes a jamming crack for the final few metres, finishing on Krokodil-hyllan. The route has good holds but few rests.
The roof left of Ullmerkotten.
Climb out along the crack through the roof and into the overhanging corner. Follow the crack to a pine, then climb easily to the top.
Start inside the corner and follow the right-hand crack on rounded holds to Krokodilhyllan.
The route is essentially a boulder problem with three hard moves. Easy climbing leads to the overhang, followed by a short but brutal section. Easier climbing follows.
Follow a crack through the roof to the top. The route can be clean-aided using only nuts.
Höstsonaten with an alternative finish to the left.
A strenuous boulder problem.

1. 6- 10m Climb first to the bolt, then follow a shallow crack to the ledge.

2. 7- 35m From the birch, move right. Climb onto a ledge and diagonally right until you reach a sharp finger crack, which is followed to the top.
Alternative finish to Varanen.

Alternative finish to Varanen. Above the roof, climb straight up a corner, over the small roof and onto the slab (crux). There is a high risk of hitting a ledge if you fall at the crux, as the last protection is quite far below. Easier climbing then leads towards the top.
Boulder problem.
Climb over the hollow, then follow the crack to Krälande krokodilen.
Enjoyable roof climbing on good holds. From Kajmaanen, climb over the roof into a small corner. Then move left and continue straight up into Varanen.
The roof is the crux. This is followed by 5 metres of strenuous but easier crack climbing. The rest of the route offers fine corner and crack climbing.
Often finished at the anchor of Inte netts.

Apart from an unexpected ‘15-metre walk’ in the middle, the first two pitches consist mainly of hand traversing, at times exposed. Large nuts should be carried.

1. 4 10m Fine hand traverse to a good belay on the ledge.

2. 4+ 30m Continue traversing past an exposed section to a ledge. Then crawl around the corner to the belay.

3. 3+ 25m From a large pine, climb to a flake. Follow its right edge.
The route starts at the finish of Stor och blaut. From there, traverse left across Varanen and continue to a good ledge. From the ledge, follow the crack diagonally up right.
The overhanging wall left of Stor och blaut. Rivets, heads, small cams and a little ingenuity.
The large, obvious chimney.
Climb over the small roof at the start, then straight up slightly left of the pillar. Move right after the crux. Needs additional bolts.
The obvious crack through the roof right of Ståre Sture. Some grade 4 free climbing on the finish.
Roof traverse above the lunch ledge.
Climb a few metres to the overhang and move left. An ‘ape-like’ move reaches a hold some way above the roof, where a nut should be placed before continuing to the top.
About 2 metres above the roof on Hoppande tallen, traverse left to a corner. Follow the corner to the top.

A key hold has broken off since the first ascent. Easy climbing leads to a prominent crack beginning above a roof. Follow the crack for a couple of metres to a small roof. Climb over the roof and continue to the top.
Starts about 5 metres right of Åteln.
Finish variation to the right above the roof.
The pillar to the left of Lederleppen.
The obvious corner.
Start up a steep corner a few metres right of Lederleppen. Move left onto the ledge, then continue slightly right and up. Follow the rib and finish up a small corner.
Follow the thin crack. The crux is a delicate balancing move.
Climb straight up the wall towards a shallow, rounded recess. Continue up this and on to Fikahyllan.
A beginner's route that is somewhat difficult to protect. Climb the wall to a crack and follow it straight up to a ramp. Continue right along the ramp and finish as for Dammiga strutsen.
Crux at the start, easier above.

This classic is the left-hand of the two most prominent lines on Vita Väggen. Start by climbing over the roof, then follow the crack all the way up. The start requires some thought. If the start proves too difficult, traverse in from the left above the crux via a ramp. Pleasant, well-protected grade 4- climbing follows.
Start as for Dammiga strutsen, but immediately above the roofs traverse right for about 6 metres. Then continue straight up the crack.
Start as for Dammiga strutsen, but immediately above the roofs traverse right all the way to Talgoxen.
Start in the recess. Pass the overhang and continue up to the traverse on Albatrossen.
Alternative start to Flyttsträcket.
Start just right of Brant och tunn, then climb diagonally right past two bolts. The crux is at the top of the steep wall. The rest of the route is considerably easier.
Follow the thin crack. Harder than it looks.
The pillar to the right of Broilern. Easier if you keep left.
Follow the thin crack to the right of Broilern. Then arc right around a spruce before continuing straight up.
The route starts by Sopa Zeke. Traverse left along a ramp, then climb into the corner and follow it to the top.

The route starts some way up to the right, on a ledge by two fallen trees. Climb the low-angled corners, cross a couple of ledges and continue into a steep corner with a thin crack on the right (crux). Follow the crack up.
Out on the slab at the far right. Film 2-Väggen.
Alternative start and finish to Orkaborka.
Perhaps ought to have its own anchor.
Climb the corner on the right-hand side of the wall to a ledge. From here, either continue straight up the crack or move a metre or so left and climb up the wall.
Short corner to the right of Teachers special.
The route starts by a large spruce, just left of the wall's highest point. Climb the large flakes and the corner, then continue left for about 20m to a large ledge.
Cross the ledge, trending right, to the next wall. Climb straight up on good holds to the top.
Climb the crack and flakes to the right of Kapritjosa. Finish up a delicate friction slab to the top.
The route needs a light clean and a top anchor.
Rebolted and brushed. Top anchor.
Branches right between the second and third bolts of Svälj huvet. Shares a top anchor with Svälj huvet.
Follow the two parallel cracks to a roof, where you can go either left or right. The right-hand option is easier if you are short. Protection is a little sparse. Top anchor.
The route has been rebolted and cleaned. Could become a classic. (Does this refer to Zeunerts röd??)
Believed to start in the thin crack, with one bolt near the end.
Could become a classic.
Starts in the thin crack.
Starts to the left of the diagonal crack on Sprickan.
A variation of Sprickan. Top anchor. (It may be to the right of Sprickan; reports differ.)
Naturally protected. A future classic.
At the far left of the wall.
To the right of Aquaminerale.
At the far right, just where you step up, left of the scruffy crack.
The right-hand transverse crack, starting just where the fixed ropes bring you onto the ledge.
Start slightly right of the line directly below the crack. Climb a few metres, then traverse left to reach the crack. Continue straight up, trending right.
To the right of Ökad tillgänglighet.
The arete at the far left of the wall. Poorly protected.
To the right of Mårtens überhang.
Between Great lake flake and Hejsan hoppsan.
The crack that leans well left at the upper left of the wall.
The shallow recess a few metres right of Diggelo diggelej.
Thin crack.
Starts behind a spruce tree by a slab standing on the ground. Climb the slab, then continue left and follow the thin crack all the way up.
Starts at the same place as Bill och Bull, but goes straight up the corner and the larger crack instead.
Starts in a prominent corner. Traverse left to reach the next obvious corner and follow it to the top.

About 15 metres right of the angle in the wall is a corner on an edge. Climb the short wall to the edge and corner, then continue up the corner to the ledge. From roughly the middle of the ledge, continue up a shallow groove to a block. Move slightly right, then straight up to the top.

Start in the largest and most obvious corner on the whole wall. The right-hand wall of the corner is fine yellowish rock. After a few metres in the corner, move left to avoid the overhang, then go straight up to the ledge. From the right-hand end of the ledge, climb a short wall and continue onto the shallow but distinct rib, following it all the way up.
Climb the corner and finish up the fine crack on the left.

Start in the corner furthest right and follow it towards the roof. The final section to the roof is somewhat difficult to protect. Traverse slightly right beneath the roof, pull over it and continue towards the top.

Start in a groove right of an obvious crack and climb it for a few metres. Follow a prominent crack left, round a bulge and continue straight up to the small ledge below the corner. Follow the corner. Where it bends right, make a short traverse right to reach the crack, then follow the crack all the way up.
Start in the obvious crack and follow it straight up to the overhang. Pull through the overhang, then move left beneath the next overhang and continue up to reach the fine crack leading to the ledge.

Starts in the middle of the wall and follows the widest crack. Climb to a pocket, continue slightly right, then move onto the wall left of the crack. Continue straight up to a small ledge, then follow the fine crack all the way up.
Climb the fine thin crack until it ends, then continue straight up to the finishing crack of Lill-Babs.
Climb the prominent flake until it ends. Move left, then continue straight up the wall to the ledge.
Climb the roof and corner formations at the far right of the wall. Where they end, move left and upwards along thin but distinct cracks.
The obvious groove on the wall above the belay ledge for Lill-Babs.
Across the slab and up into the chimney.

Agnesberg is best known for ice climbing beside Route 45. On the other side of Ramnebacken, however, there is also summer climbing. The routes were mostly climbed in the 1980s and nowadays are visited only by curious local climbers wondering what the crag has to offer.

body.text: Agnesberg is most often associated with ice climbing beside Route 45. However, summer climbing can be found on the other side of Ramnebacken. The routes were climbed mainly in the 1980s and today are visited only by curious local climbers wondering what is on offer.
The crag may be an option if you live very nearby, your car breaks down and all the trams have stopped running, while the weather still permits climbing.......
It is reportedly quite overgrown in front of the routes..

description.text: =Problems=
Sit start beneath the small overhang. Climb via the edges and the arete. Small, sharp holds.
Sit start at the arete, then follow it straight up.
Dyno from the edges left of the arete to the lip.
Standing start at the small roof; climb straight up.
Start on the jug beneath the small roof, traverse right around the arete, then climb up.
Standing start with your right hand on the sidepull and left hand on an edge; climb straight up.

A number of icefalls form on the blasted rock faces beside road 159, on the uphill stretch from road 40 towards Angered. Not the most pleasant setting in the world, but at least you are protected by concrete barriers. Park sensibly nearby – obviously not on the motorway, duh... Watch out for missing manhole covers hidden beneath the snow when walking in the ditch below the crag.

body.text: A number of icefalls adorn the blasted rock faces beside road 159, on the hill up from road 40 towards Angered. Not the most pleasant setting in the world, but at least you are protected by concrete barriers. Park sensibly nearby—obviously not on the motorway, duh... Watch out for missing manhole covers beneath the snow when walking in the ditch below the crag.
Several long, nearly vertical routes. Rarely leadable, but top-rope opportunities are not uncommon. The easiest way up is via a crawling ramp on the right-hand side.

There are some access issues at Agnesberg. Park down by Steken, where there are usually plenty of spaces. Alternatively, park up in Hammarkullen and walk down. Keep off the road and NEVER stop (or park) along Angeredsleden. The police usually receive several calls every year from annoyed or concerned motorists.
4–5 routes, most of them fairly steep. A slabby variation in the corner a little way to the right.
A few pictures so that anyone interested can see just how scenic the crag is.

description.text: Fine, sustained and long climbing directly beneath the power lines. Often quite thin.

Welcome to Borås and Almenäs! After many years of searching the woods around the town, we have finally found something resembling a bouldering area. Some of the boulders are a little low, but others would be classics even in Fontan. Perhaps a bit too steep for frog-eating slab specialists, but in our opinion, really enjoyable problems. http://www.crimp.se/web/topos/almenas/ALMENAS_BoulderGuide.pdf
0 documented routes and problems.

From Funäsdalen, drive to and past the ski areas in Tänndalen. One kilometre after the last lifts, there is a sign for ‘Naturrastplats’ on the right; park here in a suitable spot. The waterfall is clearly visible from the road; the approach is 100 metres.

body.text: From Funäsdalen, drive to and past the ski resorts in Tänndalen. One kilometre after the last lifts, there is a sign for "Naturrastplats" on the right; park here in a suitable place. The waterfall is clearly visible from the road, with a 100-metre approach.

Very easy climbing, but difficult to rig a top rope without ice screws or a long (20-metre) rope for the rigging. Bear in mind that in summer this is a real waterfall, with a very large volume of water flowing behind the ice.

description.text: Amaliagrottan is a large boulder cave in the forests of Småland, surrounded by many myths and legends. But for us climbers, all that naturally fades into insignificance when faced with the mighty crack splitting the 10-metre roof above the cave. The crack had been known to local climbers since the 1990s, but it was not until 2011 that the line was finally climbed, after a hectic couple of weeks with numerous visits by various climbers.

body.text: Amaliagrottan is a large boulder cave in the forests of Småland, surrounded by many myths and legends. For climbers, however, all that naturally pales into insignificance at the sight of the mighty crack splitting the 10-metre-long roof above the cave. The crack had been known to local climbers since the 1990s, but the line was not climbed until 2011, after a hectic couple of weeks with numerous visits from various climbers.

The landowner is supportive of climbing, but because this is a heritage site, the relevant government authorities have yet to reach a decision. It is therefore extremely important that we behave responsibly in the cave. Do not establish any new routes; nothing else in the vicinity comes close to the quality of Slagsmålsklubben. Don’t drive like rally drivers! Keep the cave clean and tidy: no tick marks and no litter!
There is a shelter by the car park. If you use it, keep a low profile.

From Eksjö: Take road 40 east towards Vimmerby and Västervik. Continue towards them through Mariannelund. Shortly after the turn-off for Pelarne and Bullerbyn, halfway down the hill, turn left towards Djurstorp (10.5 km after the level crossing in Mariannelund). Follow the road past some fine boulders and across a small stream, then turn left shortly after the stream. Keep a low profile when driving through the farm at Björnebro. If memory serves, it is signposted for fishing and follows an orange-waymarked hiking trail. Continue towards Försjön. After just under 2 kilometres, Försjön appears, with a car park and outdoor toilet on the right and a shelter and fire pit on the left. Park there and follow the path along the south-eastern (left-hand) side of the lake until you reach Amaliagrottan; it is signposted from the car park.

description.text: The obvious crack splitting the roof for at least 10 metres. Stamina and big fists will help.
0 documented routes and problems.

=General= A fairly good but isolated boulder a little too far from Umeå. Developed mainly by Henrik Johansson and Dag Knutsson. =Directions= Take road 363 towards Tavelsjö/Vindeln. Four kilometres after passing Tavelsjö, there is a turning for Rödåsel on the left; continue along road 363 for another 1 km. Park in a lay-by below a clear-felled area on the right. The boulder, which is visible from the top, lies 200 metres up the clear-felled area. =Problems=
Start on the large hold shared with “This Side”, launch left to the ramp, then head up.

Sit start beneath the roof. You can start on practically anything, but there is only one half-decent hold—in the crack—and that is the intended start. Make a long move to the jug, then continue up via slopers. No monkey slap out left. Tough.

Start standing in the middle of the boulder’s “Africa side”. The line trends right rather than left, so the ramp at the top left is off route. It does not look that hard, but this problem has prompted an astonishing amount of swearing.
Start in a half-crouch beneath the roof and follow the arete up. Big holds and lovely heel hooks—a brilliant problem. The starting holds will be marked, but use common sense until then =)

Start on the small edge on the wall beneath the roof. Continue via a tiny undercling in the roof for the left hand and a sloping ramp for the right. The arete to the left is out, should you somehow end up climbing that far left. Bloody lovely finishing move.

A brilliant dynamic variation on Phantasmagoria. Start on the same edge and dyno to the top. There are no specific exclusions, but as on Phantasmagoria, the arete to the left is out—fairly obvious. After the crux, the rules are less strict.
Very simple rules: jump to the sloping edge and get up however you can... Mantling with a capital M.
Start at the eastern corner of the boulder and traverse right to Phantasmagoria, then head up. A slopey, skin-shredding traverse. Grazes and a serious ab workout thrown in.
Super-low hanging start on two poor slopers, then brace your core and pull like mad for the next rubbish hold.
Traverse from left to right along the ramp. Climb onto the boulder where the ramp ends. Easier than it looks.
Sit start behind the uprooted tree. Straight up, no tricks.
A small crag at Askimsbadet, 8–9 metres high and 30 metres wide. 4–5 lines.

body.text: A small crag by Askimsbadet, 8–9 metres high and 30 metres wide. 4–5 lines.

Patrik Alseby climbed a few routes here in 1991. He showed the crag to a few people, but it fell into obscurity. However, if you are at Askimsbadet for a swim, you may at least want to have a look at the crag, even if you do not intend to climb any of the routes.

Askimsbadet is a municipal bathing area with modern facilities, including an accessible bathing jetty, changing rooms, showers, crazy golf and more. The crag is 100 metres from the beach and visible from it, although most of the crag is hidden by dense woodland, so you are unlikely to be disturbed by other bathers.

Drive to Askimsbadet and park. From the car park, pass the red building marked "Askims bangolf och beachcafé", go up to and through the stone-walled terrace on the left, then continue into the woods, heading towards the vertical wall visible from the beach about 100 metres away.
The crag is not described in any modern guidebook. However, Patrik Alseby probably made the first ascents of most of the lines.

description.text: Slightly overhanging climbing on small holds. The crag is not very high, but has many good routes, particularly in the higher grades. Open setting and close to swimming.

body.text: Slightly overhanging climbing on small holds. The crag is not very high, but has many fine routes, particularly in the upper grades. Open setting and close to swimming.

From Stockholm: take Värmdöleden (road 222), turn off towards "Skuru/Ektorp/Sjukhus" (before Skurubron), then turn left and pass under the motorway. Continue straight along Skurusundsvägen; after a while, the crag is on the right, immediately beside the road. Park in the car park next to the crag.
Left wall

description.text: A hard-to-grade boulder route by the pine tree.
Short and slightly awkward.
Now goes straight up to its own anchor.
Extended slightly in 2013 and finishes at the same anchor as Knutby.
Long moves between poor holds. Anchor.
Starts by the flake. Straight up, then right and up the slab. Anchor.
Starts on Frontpage, then follows the bolted line to the right.
Intense climbing. Much better than it looks.
The corner.
Immediately right of the corner.
The overhang on the prow, often confused with the route above.
A good route on unusual holds. Formerly mixed, now fully bolted.
A heavily overhanging start.
Physical. Extended by a couple of metres in 2013 and equipped with a proper anchor.
A descriptive name – the obvious crimp.
Difficult to protect.
Good moves. Anchor.
Starts immediately right of the tree. Rebolted and extended in 2013; it now goes all the way to the top.
Rebolted in 2013 and extended by a couple of metres.
The right-hand crack. Now shares an anchor with 2:an.

A 6–8-metre icefall with good, thick ice, WI3–WI4. There is also a taller, 14–18-metre icefall with steep sections, ledges, small pillars and curtains. The icefall is visible from the south side of the lake. Trees at the top can be used for abseiling and setting up a top rope. The falls face south-south-east, so the sun eats away at them badly.

Approach across the ice, or past the bathing area and summer cottages.

Långa Rumpskrivdonet (As-penn), approx. WI3, approx. 15 m. There is a large pine at the top for building a belay or abseiling from.

--Bultfeber 30 January 2011 at 17:56 (CET)

body.text:
A 6–8 metre icefall with good, thick WI3–WI4 ice, plus a taller 14–18 metre fall with steep sections, ledges, small pillars and curtains. The icefall is visible from the southern side of the lake. Trees at the top for abseiling and top-roping. The falls face south-south-east, so the sun hits them hard.

Approach across the ice or past the bathing area and summer cottages.

Långa Rumpskrivdonet (As-penn), approx. WI3, approx. 15 m; there is a large pine at the top for building a belay or abseiling from.

--Bultfeber 30 January 2011 at 17.56 (CET)
Some care should be taken, as birds of prey usually nest nearby. Take care from early March onwards—if any climbable ice forms, that is =)

description.text: Bouldering crag outside Slite on Gotland. Potential for many good problems. Visby Klätterklubb is currently developing the crag. Some loose rock.

body.text: Bouldering crag outside Slite on Gotland, with potential for many fine problems. Visby Klätterklubb is working to develop the crag. The rock is somewhat loose.

The crag lies within a Natura 2000 area. Use the gravel track to reach the crag, both to avoid disturbing wildlife or damaging the flora and because the ground is marshy at times. As usual, do not leave any litter and be considerate of the grazing sheep near the crag.

A few kilometres north of Slite, turn right towards Hellvi. After about two kilometres, turn right towards Barläst. Then follow the signs for Asunden (pay attention, as the sign comes up quickly). The final section to Asunden is very potholed. Park in the car park and walk the final stretch, a pleasant walk of about 1 km. Use the footpath.

Media:Asunden_Topo.pdf
Just north of the main crag is a small crag with a fine roof. No problems have yet been established here, but it is only a matter of time.
South of the Southern Roof is a series of smaller roofs extending to the end of the crag.

description.text: The hold-rich vertical wall to the left of Norra taket. Take any line and top out.
The obvious corner on Fyraväggen. Straight up and top out.
At the far right of Norra taket. Loose start, then straight up. Top out through the small notch in the roof.
As the name suggests: left, right, twice, then top out.
Maximilian Lund has a project at the start of Stora taket.
The arete at the right-hand edge of the large roof in the middle of the crag. Sit start, then straight up and round the roof. A pleasant, easy problem on good holds throughout.
The left-hand side of the roof. Straight up on good holds. Top out straight up, not via the vertical wall to the left.
Description to follow.
Description to follow.
Midway between Five finger and Hedlunds, this problem goes straight up. Top out.
Description to follow.
Description to follow.
Description to follow.
Description to follow.
The small roof far to the right. Sit start, continue up to the jug beneath the roof, then straight up.

A pleasant crag above the lake, with mostly bolted routes but also some trad. Amenable grades. Take a dip after a day’s climbing, either from the jetty below the crag or at the beach at the northern end of the lake. Take your rubbish home!

body.text: A pleasant crag above the lake, mostly bolted but with some trad as well. Accessible grades. Take a dip after a day’s climbing, either from the jetty below the crag or at the beach at the northern end of the lake. Take your rubbish with you!

A crag with slightly longer (20-metre) vertical routes and steep slabby routes; most are easier than 7a. Much of the crag is fairly shady. It is very popular, and there are often queues for the best routes. Take your rubbish with you. Be considerate of other climbers. Always use your own karabiners in the lower-off anchors!

Many beginners climb at Fikahyllan.

Leave the motorway at Hulanmotet and drive alongside the motorway. At the roundabout, turn left towards Aspenäs, then left again. Continue taking a left whenever possible. Follow Goråsvägen. After passing an old barn on each side of the road, a gravel car park appears on the right. Park here!
Take the train to Aspedalen, then book a taxi to the crag.

The wall furthest from the car park. To get here, follow the road until the crag ends, then enter the woods. An approximately 20-metre-high wall with several bolted routes. The best is probably the route sharing the wall’s name, Nalle Puh (5c).
Located above Nalle Puh. Take care not to kick down stones or drop anything on climbers at Nalle Puh.
Right of the Nalle Puh area, or directly below Highwayhyllan. Some harder routes, including Sheriffen.

Highwayhyllan is recommended! The easiest approach is to scramble up above Fikahyllan and follow the top of the crag until you see the two lower-off anchors for Highway 66 and Sonic Youth. There are two glue-in bolts at the base of the sport routes. Take care not to kick down stones onto Sheriffen, and remember to shout “Rock!”.
Above Fikahyllan. Scramble down, or climb up via one of the routes between Svarta leden and Mr100.
Do not top-rope directly through the rings! Use your own karabiners. From the car park, enter the woods and head uphill, parallel to the road.

Right of the car park, rather than left where all the other routes are. Five or six bolted routes of average quality, equipped with lower-off anchors. Recommended on hot summer days when Fikahyllan is too warm, or when all the other sectors at Aspen are packed with climbers (which does happen).

description.text: A more frequently climbed route.
Starts as for Nalle Puh, then continues to the anchor right of Nalle Puh's.
Tiger finishes at Nasse's anchor.
The chimney right of Plåtniklas. The bolts are entirely avoidable (same grade, more enjoyable climbing).
Not entirely unclimbed.
Now bolted? What is its name?
Tv1 and Tv2 are the inviting cracks left of the overhang (Robin Hood).
Super Channel is the continuation of Tv1/Tv2.
The very inviting overhang.
Follows the flake.
Starts as for Sheriffen, but goes straight up.
Ends at the ring.
A continuation of Little John; it should be climbed from the ground, not from a hanging belay.
Flakmoppen's finish is called Afrodite; Afrodite continues and becomes Helenas salva.
There are two top-rope anchors left of Mors lilla olle, with individual bolts.
The anchors are quite easy to see from the foot of Fikahyllan.
Climbs to the final bolt.
A continuation of Mors lilla Olle.
The crack directly below Mors lilla Olle's anchor, with an old piton four metres up.
The bolts have now been removed.
Exposed. Highway 66 is the arete with an abseil anchor.
The well-protected crack. An excellent beginner's route!
Starts in the corner and follows the crack. Some loose holds and plenty of dirt. Dirty finish. The route gets no stars.
The offwidth crack starting as for Carl Gustavs HP, but going straight up.
A fine route.
Easy to rig a top rope if you have a static rope. A fine, beginner-friendly route.
A very fine beginner's route.
A sport route, regardless of what the guidebook says. Very fine!
A top-rope route that goes straight up to the anchor.
Not recommended.
The corner four metres right of Caffé Latte.
Abseil anchor on a small wall left of Mumindalen.
0 documented routes and problems.
A small crag that no one climbs at. There are three bolted routes.

body.text: A small crag that no one climbs at. There are three bolted routes.

Akkas gata is probably the nearest/best stop. However, the crag is easier to find from Olshammarsgatan. From the Olshammarsgatan stop, walk up Plockerotsvägen, then turn right into the woods (towards the meadow you have just passed). The crag soon comes into view.

Map here. Click the map button above.

Add directions here.

(The crag is described very vaguely in Göteborg med omnejd)

description.text: Route description

Comment: An aid highball, if you like; not really worth bothering with. Climbs left of the ‘big’ wall, between the offwidth crack and the ‘one-bolt route’. Thin cracks, good edges and possibly a birch tree. Starts with bodyweight placements. Unfortunately(?) looks easier to free-climb than to aid. The following gear was used: 2 aluheads (5–6 mm), 1 birdbeak, 1 BD Grappling Hook, 1 Camalot #1, 1 Lost Arrow #1. Location: Backadalenklippan (under ‘other crags’ in the book).
A truly mythical glacial erratic. Approximately 4–5 metres high and, amusingly, featuring every angle of climbing (slab, vertical and overhanging) on a single boulder.

body.text: A truly mythical giant-thrown boulder. Estimated to be 4–5 metres high and, amusingly, it features every type of climbing terrain—slab, vertical and overhanging—on a single boulder.

A more up-to-date guide is available at www.27crags.com due to problems updating this guide.

Generally flat, good landings. Only the odd small rock, stump or bit of scrub gets in the way.

The boulder has probably been climbed before, but this has not been verified. Please correct the page directly if the problem names, grades, etc. are inaccurate.

While I was there, I spoke to the landowner, Nils, who lives at the end of the gravel road. He not only gave his permission but also encouraged visits and climbing. He even offered to lend me ladders if I needed help getting up. He also said he had some boulders on his property that I was welcome to visit. I went up for a look, but the ones I saw were not interesting.

The size of the boulder means that many different problems of varying character can be established. There is probably room for 10–15 individual problems. The potential for new lines is considerable, so the boulder is categorised as unclimbed for anyone on the hunt.

A small pine grows on top of the boulder. An old rope or line has been attached to it, which tourists and others have used together with the adjacent spruce to get onto the boulder. The condition of the rope is of course unknown, but it can be useful for descending. The descent may otherwise prove problematic, and bringing your own rope to attach somewhere is not a bad idea.

Note that the boulder is a local attraction and receives regular visitors. The County Administrative Board has also classified it as some kind of protected site. Act accordingly!

The boulder is mostly shaded throughout the day. The back—the overhang—and the top may therefore remain particularly damp for several days after rain. Watch out for slippery moss on slabby top-outs and when descending! Several times trickier than the climb up.

From Arvika:

Leave Arvika on Road 175 towards Klässbol and Säffle. After just under 20 km, turn right towards Klässbol. After another few hundred metres, you can once again turn down right towards Klässbol. At this four-way junction, instead turn left towards Värmskog, Avelsbol, or whatever the signs happen to say. Continue along this road for about 8 km without leaving the tarmac. Look out for an ancient monument sign pointing uphill to the right. If you reach a T-junction overlooking a lake, you have driven all the way to Värmskog. In that case, find Liljenäs café and try one of their wonderful prawn sandwiches—the one with fresh prawns. Then turn around and continue looking.

From Karlstad:

Leave Karlstad on the E18 towards Oslo. At the roundabout by Grums—Burger King and the OK petrol station—turn right towards Grums Centrum. Before reaching the centre, turn right towards Värmskog, by the riding school and a burger kiosk. Follow this road for a few kilometres. Turn right towards Värmskog again, followed almost immediately by another left turn towards Värmskog. Cross the bridge into Värmskog and continue through it. At a left-hand bend around Lake Lilla Värmeln, turn uphill to the right towards Klässbol, Avelsbol, or whatever the sign happens to say. There is a small fire station on the bend. Follow this road for about 10 km until you see a heritage-site sign pointing uphill to the left. If you reach a T-junction, you have gone much too far. Turn around and get it right.

Parking

Park in a small lay-by just before the boulder.

See the images below for the shape of the boulder.

Solid lines = completed problems

Dashed lines = projects, cleaned to varying degrees.

Heavily covered in lichen and moss that has become part of the rock. There are no obvious holds, but there is probably something small enough beneath the moss. This could yield some fun, balancy lines requiring full-body contact with the rock. There may also be something where Side 1 meets Side 4.
Easier terrain, with ledges on the vertical section leading into a slab. Show the utmost consideration when climbing on this side, as the road runs immediately beside the boulder.

The boulder’s highest face, with a full height estimated at 4–5 metres. If anyone manages to climb this completely blank face, I will be the first to take my hat off to them. The face is entirely smooth, with microscopic ripples. A highball and a true testpiece.
Fairly steeply overhanging, mainly with poor holds, leading to a slabby lip and top-out. Testpiece!

description.text: The problem nearest the camera in the photo. Climbs more or less straight up.
The problem in the middle of the photo. Needs cleaning.
The furthest problem.
The project on the right is the one being climbed in the photo.
An interesting problem on the arete.
Might a strong climber make easy work of the project shown in the photo?

A crag on Lidingö, opposite Värtahamnen. It is low (6–7 m), but the routes are sustained and entertaining. The crag has a number of short, slightly overhanging problems. All routes are bolt-protected. Some of the bolts have been painted in an attempt to blend them into the colour of the rock. Watch out for pine needles on the top-outs by the large pine; it may be worth brushing these before climbing.

body.text: A crag on Lidingö opposite Värtahamnen. It is low (6–7 m), but the routes are sustained and enjoyable. The crag has several short, slightly overhanging problems. All routes are protected by bolts. Some of the bolts have been painted in an attempt to blend them into the colour of the rock. Watch out for pine needles at the top-outs by the large pine tree; it may be worth brushing these top-outs before climbing them.
South-west facing and relatively open.

Go to the Baggeby residential area (see map). Find Farkostvägen 8. Opposite this block of flats, a clear gravel path leads down towards the water. Follow it as it bends right for about 3 minutes. The crag is on the right-hand side, on a small slope.

description.text: Start up Klifingertango, then head right at the second bolt.
Start up Klifingertango, then head right at the first bolt.
Without the rock.

Note that the ice is on a south-facing slope, so conditions may be poor unless it has been properly cold for a couple of days.

For current ice conditions or other enquiries, contact Sundsvalls Klätterklubb (http://www.sundsvallsklatterklubb.se).

body.text:
Note that the icefalls are on a south-facing slope, so conditions may be poor unless it has been really cold for a couple of days.

For current ice conditions or other enquiries, contact Sundsvalls Klätterklubb (http://www.sundsvallsklatterklubb.se).

Take road 631/320 to Laggaberg, between Timrå and Kovland. Turn off towards Laggarberg (the crag is now visible to the north), keep left and park near the end of the road.

The approach takes about 20–30 minutes, depending on snow conditions. Walk straight towards the crag and follow the edge until you find the icefalls. If you reach the tarn, you have gone too far.
Several small icefalls, with a few freestanding pillars in varying condition.
There are currently no access issues, but when parking, make sure you do not obstruct passing traffic.

description.text:
A pleasant collection of boulders outside Örebro, mostly with good rock quality. There is something for everyone here: plenty of easy problems, a couple of unrepeated testpieces and some hard projects.

The main attraction is undoubtedly Broccoliblocket, a large, impressive overhanging boulder with a few fun projects and problems. Please note that attempts to contact the landowner have been unsuccessful, so park considerately and preferably stay away during the moose-hunting season!

body.text:
A pleasant collection of boulders outside Örebro, mostly with good rock quality. There is something for everyone here: lots of easy problems, a couple of unrepeated testpieces and some hard projects.

The main attraction is undoubtedly Broccoliblocket, a large, impressive overhanging boulder with a few fun projects and problems. Please note that attempts to contact the landowner have been unsuccessful, so park considerately and preferably stay away during the elk-hunting season!

From Örebro, take the E18 towards Karlskoga, then turn onto road 204 towards Fjugesta. A little past Mullhyttan, turn right towards Torhyttan. Follow the signs for Torhyttan for about 4 km, then turn left towards Ölsdalen. Do not take the first left after about 200 metres. People have taken the wrong turning here! After 400 metres, you will reach a fork. Park here or, if you feel ‘authorised’, continue for about 400 metres to the parking spot shown in the photo below, by the clear-cut area.
