Semesterblocket is visible from the road. All projects are open! Get out there and send!

Semesterblocket

The first boulder to be developed. Mostly lowballs where the start usually determines the grade. There is also a brushed project for anyone keen to have a go.

description.text: Sit-start beneath the arete. Right hand on the sloping edge and left on one of the spikes to the left. Right foot on the large, obvious foothold low and right. Make a huge throw to the sloper high above your head, or finesse your way up along the edge. Keep your abs tight!
Sit-start with an angled edge for the right hand and a sloper for the left. Find something to push off and dyno to the bobbly holds. Or finesse your way up, of course... Hard.

It looks neither hard nor good, but it is both! The problem is balancy, crimpy and fun. Start on the obvious loaf-shaped hold and continue to the crimps. Bring the left foot up high, squeeze hard and mantle out.
Standing start on the super-easy, slabby problem right of the pine tree. The start is probably harder if you are short.

Standing start via the jugs, then onto the ledge and on to the top. For a slightly burlier experience at around 6A+, try a sit-start from the large, obvious hold beneath the ledge, using the pleasant little flexing flake.
Sit-start with the left hand in a tiny crack and the right on a knob. Straight up. Hard to grade.
Sit-start with the left hand in the lovely, obvious pocket and the right on a sideways crimp far to the right. Preferably with your legs spread wide.

Sit-start beneath the projecting arete, slap your way up to either the left or right, then mantle right of the projecting ‘nose’. Probably easier if you are short and do not have to wrestle with your legs.

A potentially pleasant warm-up. Sit-start with your feet on the flakes and hands on the edge, then traverse around the first corner. Tricky to bring your feet round, at least if you are tall. Rest briefly, then drop down, climb beneath the ‘nose’ and mantle out on The jakttower without getting too pumped.

Traverse the inviting edge across the front of the small boulder. Sit-start with the right hand in the crack at the far right of the boulder, move left and mantle out at the far end. Watch out for midges!
Sit-start as for Knottkaos, but make an easy mantle straight up using the thin crack system. Watch out for mosquitoes!

Sit-start as for Män som hatar crimpar. Once you have pinched the crimp rail with your left hand, bring up your right toe and throw your right hand out to the right side of the arete. Then work your way up to the jug on this side! Fun!

Sit-start beneath the arete. Heave round to the left onto the crimp rail, then up onto the obvious ledge. A really good problem on a striking feature. The standing start is also good and probably at least 5A.

Standing start from two crimps in the middle of the wall. Stay right of the crack near the top of the boulder! Crimp your way up to the pleasant sloping edge and finish at the horizontal cracks above the sloper.
Standing start right of the broccoli arete. Climb the boulder on good holds.
Standing start on the bobbly arete. Good starting holds, then up to slopers. Anyone fancy the burly sit-start?
A traverse starting on Broccoli, continuing around the entire front of the boulder and finishing up In a Flash. A good problem and a good warm-up. The shuffling crux is extremely addictive!

Start with both hands on the obvious hold at the end of the crack near the top of the boulder. Make a long move to good holds at the end of the crack, avoiding the large holds on the small boulder to the right.

Sit-start with the right hand in the crack beneath the arete and the left on a pinch. Fold the pad in half or start hanging if you cannot reach. For the full experience, try to top out straight up rather than to the left. The standing start is around 5C/6A.

An unrepeated and hard low start to Axsjön directe. Start beneath the boulder with the left hand in the horizontal crack and the right on a sidepull/pinch. Toe-hook the roof with the left foot and smear the right foot on the edge, then continue into the regular problem.

Another unrepeated tough one. Start from the obvious flake beneath the overhang, with your hands on the flake and feet in the roof. Traverse via vicious crimps on the slightly loose block and a toe lock on the arete to the left, into Axsjön Directe and finish up that. Brutally hard and cool! The greyish rock to the left that the boulder rests on is out. Mind the back of your head at the start!
Sit-start on top of the boulder left of Flapper-SM, with the left hand in a good, slightly hidden crack and the right on any old rubbish. Aim up and left for the fork, then mantle out.
Squeeze in between the rock and the tree. Standing start from two underclings, then head up the loose, sharp and rather unpleasant wall.
Sit-start behind the boulder. Dusty and a bit grim until you reach better rock and a mantle that is almost fun.

Hanging start from the obvious niche at the back of the cave. Move your hands left and into the crack in a brutally strenuous refrigerator position. Climb through the roof, out onto the lip and straight up the shield, which has now been fully brushed. It will be hard, strenuous and high! Who will be first?!

Standing start beneath the crack. Take the top of the crack with your left hand and thrutch onto the ledge for a slightly scary, somewhat mossy mantle involving awkward moves. If you cannot reach the top of the crack, you will probably need to jam a little, which is sure to raise the grade a notch or two. The climbing from the ledge to the top also puts up some resistance.

Walk 50 metres along the crag until an obvious, freshly brushed slab appears on the left. Innan regnet faller is the most obvious line up the middle, starting at the bottom of the crack formation and going straight up. A little scary, especially without a spotter. It may need a winter before all the dirt disappears.
Standing start with one rail for the feet and another for the hands. Straight up. The edge is out! Bring your feet up high.

Take the obvious jug rail with both hands. Get your feet onto the sloper and continue up. The sit-start has been done and is around 6B; it includes an extremely vicious, flesh-eating crimp on the left side of the arete. It probably does not make the problem much more fun or any better.

An extraordinary amount of climbing packed into very few centimetres. Lie on your back in the cave and grab the projecting nose. Lock your feet into the roof crack on the left and pinch the edge. Now place your left heel on the nose and power your way up, or use the edge. Gymnastic, short and fun! Give it a go!
Sit-start beneath the small arete and power your way up. Fun and a little strenuous!

Bastustainen is near Hägdarve. At Rone church, turn towards Burs. After approx. 1.5 km, turn right towards Gerete. After approx. 1 km, just before the first house on the right, a small road/path leads to the boulder.

body.text: Bastustainen is near Hägdarve. At Rone Church, turn towards Burs. After about 1.5 km, turn right towards Gerete. After about 1 km, just before the first house on the right, a small track/path leads in to the boulder.

description.text:
A small crag east of Trollbäcken. It consists of a 20 m-high slab and a smaller but overhanging side wall. There is good bouldering potential in the area (bring a cleaning brush). The crags at Tyresö-Flaten are only a few kilometres away. Bansjön, 200 m from the crag, has an excellent bathing jetty. A picnic crag!

2011-06 The crag is unclimbable, with moss and trees on the slab. Bolt in poor condition. Local climbers are planning to restore the crag.

body.text:
Small crag east of Trollbäcken. The crag consists of a 20 m slab and a smaller but overhanging side wall. There is good bouldering potential in the area (bring a cleaning brush). The crags at Tyresö-Flaten are only a few kilometres away. Bansjön, 200 m from the crag, has an excellent swimming jetty. A picnic crag!

2011-06 The crag is unclimbable, with moss and trees on the slab. Bolt in poor condition. Local climbers are planning to restore this crag.

Take Tyresövägen (road 229), but instead of heading towards Tyresö, head towards Haninge and Vendelsö along Gudöbroleden (road 260). At the second set of traffic lights, turn left onto Skogsängsvägen and continue for 1.5 km until it ends and becomes Barnsjöslingan. Park and follow the gravel path to the crag on the left.

description.text: Traverse right from Before...
Surprisingly good.

The crag is on southern Farstalandet, immediately west of Beatelunds gård. It is a fairly large crag with several different sections, some of which drop straight into Kolström, the very narrow strait between Farstalandet and Ingarö. The crag has been fairly thoroughly explored, though there is still potential for more routes.

body.text: The crag lies on southern Farstalandet, just west of Beatelund farm. It is a fairly large crag with several different sections, some dropping straight into Kolström, the very narrow strait between Farstalandet and Ingarö. The crag has been fairly thoroughly explored, though there is still potential for more routes.
A guide with a topo is available on Stockholms Nätförare (http://www.plonk.se/oldsite/beatelund.html)

Drive towards Ingarö and turn towards Beatelund before the bridge. After 1.4 km, pass an impressive gate on the left. After another 400 m, the road bends sharply right. Park here. Walk into the woods and head towards the highest point. The red wall lies directly below, with Cabragg on the right.

The starts of Kladdragg, Cabragg and Sponken are on a fairly exposed ledge. Bring something (typically a cam) to anchor the belayer and avoid a tandem fall before the first bolt is clipped.

These routes and Borsten Torsten share a two-bolt top anchor that should be replaced or altered. The hangers sit a couple of centimetres proud of the rock, with around ten washers between them and the rock. Perhaps more seriously, the ring hanger in use is not connected to the other bolt. Instead, the chain has been attached to the expansion stud beneath it (meaning that if anything happens to it, the backup also fails...).
2011-10-04. The loose nut in the anchor that was reported has now been fixed.

description.text: More like 6b.
Right of FTW.
Loads of more or less loose holds and footholds! If you cannot resist climbing the route, the belayer should wear a helmet!!
Follows the arete left of "Den maskerade..".
Short, with a distinct crux.
Powerful and overhanging.
Steep.
Difficult start with a poorly positioned bolt.
Alternative start to Bamsiga baguetter.
Superb climbing and surprisingly well protected, with a couple of small cams at the start.
Finish via Dubbla militärkallingar.
Scary protection behind flakes, none of it reliable. Worth doing on top-rope, though.

As the whole wall is climbable, you can effectively do two pleasant parallel lines. The protection is mediocre where the wall ends, but if the rope is long enough you can continue for around twenty metres to some pine trees.
Long.

This crag on the island of Södra Berghamn, off Stavsnäs, is up to 20 metres high. Easy to moderately difficult climbing. A good place for beginners, with suitable trees for setting up top ropes. The left-hand section of the crag has good-quality rock. A few routes have been climbed on top rope (approximately 6a–6c+).

body.text: This crag is on the island of Södra Berghamn, off Stavsnäs, and is up to 20 metres high. Easy and moderate climbing. A good place for beginners, with good trees for setting up top ropes. The left-hand section of the crag is good-quality rock. Some routes have been climbed on top rope (approximately 6a–6c+).
On a calm day, the boat can easily be moored directly below the crag. In summer, when there are a lot of waves or it is windy, the bay facing Norra Berghamn is a better option.

description.text: The obvious corner on the left-hand side of the crag. Crux halfway up. Protected with a couple of small cams.
Billingehus lies directly above Skövde, home to Billingens Klätterklubb.

body.text: Billingehus lies directly above Skövde and is home to Billingens Klätterklubb.

Map here

Follow the road uphill to the car park outside Billingenbadet.

Gamla liften is the part of the crag outside the nature reserve, south of the narrows, and is described in the guidebook. It begins at the ski slope and extends north towards the narrows. Quite a few routes have been established, with scope for many more.

The easiest approaches are either from below—park in the ski slope car park and walk up beneath the small lift. Enter the woods where the "Snowparken" begins and continue straight up towards the crag.

From above, park by the bandy pitch. Walk behind the hotel and descend between the ski slope and the chapel. Take care not to walk in front of the chapel’s panoramic windows!!

Two fixed ropes have been installed for ascent and descent and are easiest to locate from below. One is between routes 5 and 9, and the other between routes 35 and 41.

The first section begins immediately beside the ski slope.
Guidebook from Billingens Klätterklubb (http://www4.idrottonline.se/ImageVaultFiles/id_245724/cf_72266/forareversion1.pdf)

description.text: Well-protected route.

Balingsta is in the Orlången Nature Reserve. There is one sector on the left-hand side of the road and three on the right. Further on, at the end of the road, the fifth sector lies in the deciduous woodland by the meadows beside the lake. The rock can be a little loose on some problems. All problems are marked with a white arrow.

body.text: Balingsta lies in Orlången Nature Reserve. There is one sector on the left-hand side of the road, three on the right, and the fifth is further on at the end of the road, in the deciduous woodland by the meadows down by the lake. The rock can be a little loose on some problems. All problems are marked with a white arrow.

From central Stockholm, take Huddingevägen (226), turn onto Lännavägen (259), and continue for about 1 km after passing Balingsnäs. Turn left (signposted Balingsta Gård) onto a gravel road lined by a long avenue of old trees. Continue for about 950 m; there are a couple of parking bays on the left, with another couple about 200 m further on.

To reach Kvarnen, drive on for a few hundred metres and park in the large car park on the right before the stables. Walk past the stables, around the riding arena and follow the road beyond the barrier, across the fields, until you arrive.

As mentioned, the area lies within Orlången Nature Reserve, with all that this entails. Be extremely careful to take all rubbish with you and show consideration for nature and other visitors.

Note: just a warning—there can be an incredible number of mosquitoes on some days. Otherwise, it is a great place.
2008 Balingsta guidebook in PDF format (http://www.sverigeforaren.se/media/Balingsta.pdf)

The sector is on the opposite side of the road from the other sectors (the left-hand side as you drive there). Follow the path along the edge of the meadow; at the start of the path there is a sign saying "No horse riding". Walk for about 150 m until you see a split boulder—you have arrived!

It is probably best to park in the last parking bay before the field begins, on the right. Then walk back about 100 m and into the woods on the left (i.e. the right-hand side of the road as you drive in). There is a sort of path a little way into the woods, which can be hard to find the first time.
To come straight here, the easiest approach is to follow the edge of the meadow from the road.

A scenic sector with deciduous woodland, swimming and a barbecue area. The walls are extremely mosquito-ridden during the summer season. There are several fine highball problems, some so high that they have so far only been climbed on top-rope. One problem even turned out to be an old trad route. Landings are generally very good.

A small but fiercely overhanging crag in a distinctly urban setting in Karlshamn’s industrial estate. Only a handful of routes have been established, but Gelin Karelin is regarded as one of the classic hard routes in the Skåne/Blekinge region. The approach is very short – you can practically belay through the car’s sunroof! All sport routes have anchors.

body.text: A small but fiercely overhanging crag in a decidedly urban setting in Karlshamn’s industrial estate. Only a handful of routes have been established, but Gelin Karelin is considered one of the great hard classics of the Skåne/Blekinge region. The approach is extremely short – you can practically belay through the car’s sunroof! All sport routes have anchors.

From the west: Take the E22 towards Kalmar. Leave the E22 at Karlshamn västra (exit 51). Turn right at the end of the slip road. Head south on road 29 for about 1.7 km, then turn left at the next roundabout onto Vekerumsvägen. Continue for 800 metres, then turn left onto Gröna vägen. Continue for another 60 metres. You can’t miss it! It is also signposted to Bilprovningen, though not all the way from the E22.

description.text: A steep, bouldery start followed by an easier crack. Reasonably well protected, but beware the ledge. Quite short. Finishes at the pine tree in the main photo. Previously a steep A1+ aid line, Ola Modéer -08.
Good, steep aid climbing, finishing with a thin copperhead seam on a slab. There is a rivet where the seam disappears completely.

Superb, very steep sport climbing along the overhanging crack. A must for the dedicated southern Swedish sport climber! Anchor. Established by Mark Jansson in the early 90s, but Patrik Gelin made the first successful ascent. Can also be climbed on trad gear...
A solid central line, considered very good. Anchor.
A variant if you have nothing better to do – climb the first half of Japp, then move into Gelin Karelin.
The hardest route in Skåne/Blekinge! The line tackles the crag's most compact face, offering very little respite. Anchor.
A good line that is trickier than it looks. Anchor.

=General= A welcome addition to the Ludvika bouldering scene. The "area" consists of a single but excellent boulder close to town. The guide is not yet complete; more problems have been climbed and will hopefully be added here shortly. Please help refine the grades!

=Directions= Take Näsvägen out onto Biskopsnäset and park at the obvious widening in the road by the small clear-cut area. Make sure to leave enough room for the cottage owners. Take the path out to the track and follow it south. Cross to the other side of the track when possible and continue south. At the 1.5 km sign, turn off towards the lake to reach the boulder.
A little trickier than it looks. Can be climbed in either direction. Blue line.

description.text: A forgotten gem on the Södermanland coast. This 15–20-metre crag near Bistaberget has several fine aid routes, as well as around ten high-quality sport and trad routes. Several new routes, both sport and trad, were added to the main wall in 2012. In addition, several of the aid routes were freed.

body.text: A forgotten gem on the Södermanland coast. A 15–20-metre-high crag near Bistaberget, with several fine aid routes and around ten high-quality sport and trad routes. Several new routes, both sport and trad, were added to the main wall in 2012. Several of the aid routes were also freed.

A new guidebook covering Simonsberget, Bistaberget and other crags in Södermanland was published in November 2006. Order via www.dangerzone.se (http://www.dangerzone.se). The author reports that work has begun on a supplement to the guidebook.

body.text: To reach Blåbärsstenarna, take the same road as for Eskön. After turning off the old E4 towards Eskön, turn right onto a gravel road on a left-hand bend a few hundred metres from the ‘main’ road. Follow the gravel road until it forks, then take the left fork. After a couple of hundred metres, turn right onto a smaller forest road. Continue for another couple of hundred metres and the boulders will be on your left.

At the green arrow.

description.text: A long, vertical crag around 20 metres high, with some 50 routes graded from 3- to 8. Varied and enjoyable climbing.

body.text: A long, vertical crag around 20 m high, with some 50 routes ranging from grade 3- to 8. Varied and excellent climbing.

Bistaberget, in south-eastern Södermanland, lies in the old mining parish of Tunaberg, just a few kilometres from the climbing mecca of Simonsberget. Bistaberget is a long, vertical crag around 20 m high. Today there are some 50 routes here, ranging from grade 3- to 8. Many of the routes are bolted and equipped with lower-off anchors. Bistaberget also has several fine mixed routes, where the crux is bolted in most cases, while good cracks have been left for traditional protection.

Camping at Bistaberget is not particularly easy, but with a little goodwill there is room for 2–3 tents on the slabs 20 m into the woods from the top.

A guidebook covering Simonsberget, Bistaberget and other crags in Södermanland was published in November 2006. Order from Danger Zone (http://www.dangerzone.se).

During autumn 2009, several new anchors were installed and some routes were rebolted. The work will continue in early 2010. More information at http://dangerzone.forum24.se/dangerzone-about18.html.

If approaching from the north on the E4, take the Nyköping V exit—the last exit for Nyköping. At the second roundabout, turn right onto road 53 towards Oxelösund. Follow signs for Nävekvarn. In Nävekvarn, turn left at the T-junction in the village and continue for about 3.5 km. The crag is beside the road and impossible to miss.

Bear in mind that the lay-by by the crag also provides access to the field for the farmer and his tractor. Park considerately.
A guide provided by Linköpings klätterklubb is available here (http://linkopingsklatterklubb.se/index.php/forare.html).

description.text: Uncleaned solo route. Not fun!
Starts in a crack formation, continues up a small corner, via a ledge and then up the larger jamming crack. Essentially a pure trad route, but crosses the first bolt of Kapten Kroks förbannelse.
Same start as Kapten Krok, but after the ledge goes straight up past three bolts. Two finish variants, 5a and 6c.

A very good and varied route. Goes through the obvious alcove and up into a system of corners, cracks and other fun features. The second half is protected by three bolts, one piton and trad gear, with good protection.

Enjoyable, steep climbing up an obvious crack system. Starts right of the crack leading into the crack system, then traverses left into the cracks via a few crimps. Well protected. Large, sturdy pine at the top for top-roping if desired; bring a static rope.

The first half is well bolted; the second half takes trad gear. The anchor is some way left, beyond the small pine. There is also a nut-protected finish of the same grade that goes straight up at the end, climbed by Andreas Harne in September 2011. A proper abseil anchor is to be installed on the variant; currently there is a single bolt.
The grade is disputed. The guidebook gives 7b+, which is very generous. Many consider it closer to 7a+.
An enjoyable, well-bolted route, apart from the start. Exposed for the grade, with good climbing.
A very good, nut-protected finish. Follow large holds up the obvious crack.
Start up Ättestupan, then go straight up at the roof. Follow the vertical crack straight up and build an anchor on the pine. Superb, sustained climbing.
Start up Ättestupan, but immediately go dead straight up and join Lilla flickan zombie. Fantastic positions.
The extremely obvious, large crack. Not as easy as it looks, and also quite mossy.
Somewhat fingery and technical, on rock that is quite unusual for the crag: eroded, rough and limestone-like.
Södermanland’s first 8a.

Take the Ingarö exit from Route 222 (Värmdöleden). Drive through Brunn and continue straight towards Björkvik. Shortly before Björkviks brygga, turn left at the sign for Björnö Nature Reserve. The crag is on the left, 50 m before the first speed bump.

body.text: Take the Ingarö exit from route 222, Värmdöleden. Drive through Brunn and continue straight towards Björkvik. Shortly before Björkviks brygga, turn left at the sign for Björnö Naturreservat. The crag is on the left, 50m before the first speed bump.

There is another bouldering wall inside Björnö Naturreservat. Park in the large car park, then follow the road past a few houses until you reach a sharp right-hand bend. Take the gravel path downhill to the left for approx. 300m and you are there.

NOTE: This is a nature reserve. Please take your litter with you and try to remove any chalk and tick marks. Thank you.

From Arvika, take road 175 towards Klässbol/Säffle. At the southern Ingestrand turn-off, head up towards Björnmyra. Signposted from the road? The crag is on the far side of the clear-cut area you pass on your left before crossing the stream. However, this crag has seen very little traffic in recent years, so it is in great need of clearing, both at the base (very overgrown) and on the routes!

Two small walls with short, intense problems, plus a highball wall. Short approaches and close to the Tube, so definitely worth a visit. Be prepared for a few spectators at Parkeringsväggen.

body.text: Two small walls with short, intense problems, plus a highball wall. Short approaches and close to the metro, so definitely worth a visit. Expect some spectators at Parkeringsväggen.

From the city centre or the E4, take the exit leading up to the large roundabout above Nynäsvägen and turn left at the roundabout. You should now be on Sofielundsvägen. Turn left onto Garagevägen, then almost immediately right onto Paternostervägen. Follow this road as it becomes Ulricehamnsvägen. Take the next right onto Malmövägen and follow it until you see a church on the left. Turn left just after the church and park. Free parking for 4 hours.

A larger crag with a few highballs and boulder problems, as well as some brutally hard projects for those so inclined. Walk past the block of flats on the left; the crag is on the left, opposite the recreation centre. Expect a few comments from curious children.

From the car park, with the nursery on your left and the large block of flats on your right, walk straight ahead onto the path. Follow it uphill for a short distance and look left; you will see a small wall a little way into the woods. The crag has been brushed, even if it does not look like it in the photo.

Take the footpath that passes the nursery, the single-storey building that looks a little like a prefab hut. This brings you to a grass field. Continue straight ahead, cross the football pitch, and take the small path into the woods just to the left of the main floodlit trail. The crag is on the left after a few metres.

The slightly higher wall, about 10 m to the right of Tott.

=Directions= Take the E4 from Stockholm towards Södertälje and turn off at the last “Stockholm exit”, towards Alby by the Shell petrol station. Follow the old Södertäljevägen. After passing the lake, turn right towards Draget/Norsborgs Herrgård. Take the second turning on the left, signposted Vällinge. Drive almost all the way to Vällinge; the bloc is in the woods below the field, just before the Home Guard huts. Finding suitable parking is not easy. You can park by a small entrance to the field on the opposite side of the road, a short distance away, but make sure you do not block access for tractors. =Problems=
The right-hand part of the block's steep face.

Blaxsås stands at 438 metres above sea level, just north of Nianfors, or about 20 km north-west of Enånger. The summit offers what is probably one of the finest views in Hälsingland. You can see the entire archipelago from north of Hudiksvall to south of Söderhamn, as well as both Dellen lakes. The very steep east face of Blaxsås is clearly visible from a long way off. For an easy ascent, there are various routes graded 2–3. For rock climbing, the crag can be divided into 3–4 sections: Section A is about 200 m south of the shelter at the summit.

Fuskåsberget is also nearby.

body.text:
Blaxsås stands at 438 m above sea level, just north of Nianfors, around 20 km north-west of Enånger. The summit probably offers one of the finest views in Hälsingland. You can see the entire archipelago from north of Hudiksvall to south of Söderhamn, as well as both Dellensjöarna lakes. Blaxsås’s eastern face is clearly visible from a long way off as a very steep wall. For easy mountaineering, there are various routes at grade 2–3. For pure rock climbing, the crag can be divided into 3–4 sections: Section A lies around 200 m south of the shelter at the summit.

Fuskåsberget is also nearby.

Detailed directions are not available. However, the map on the right gives a rough idea—look more closely on hitta.se (http://www.hitta.se/SearchCombi.aspx?SearchType=4&wflWhite=1a1b&wflPink=4a&vad=&var=blacks%e5s). GPS coordinates for the crag: Lat: N 61º 38' 41.56" Long: E 16º 45' 59.85"
Around 200 m south of the shelter at the summit.
The southern part of the main wall. There are some fine lines, but none has yet been climbed.
The wall directly to the left below the shelter. There is plenty still to do here, at a range of grades.

description.text: Two short pitches; the first is a wide jamming crack. On the second pitch, move out right beneath a roof leading to the crux. Mantel up into a thin jamming crack, which is the crux. Exposed.

Crack, overhanging in the final section. The route is in the right-hand part of the sector. Just right of the slab is a 15–20 m chimney, which has been climbed in winter and graded 3+. Just before reaching Blaxsås, there is a crag on the right with a fine south-facing wall. A handful of routes have been climbed, but there are other fine lines. For those who enjoy crawling through caves, there is plenty to explore below the main Blaxsås wall. Numerous large boulders lie piled on top of one another there.

description.text: See also Göteborg. Sweden’s premier climbing area stretches from Göteborg in the south to the Norwegian border in the north. It offers varied climbing, from hard sport routes to superb crack lines on extremely hard granite of the highest quality. See also http://www.bohusbergen.se. Take great care to follow the established access rules when visiting Bohuslän. Access is particularly sensitive in several areas. Tread carefully, so that more people can enjoy Bohuslän’s granite. =Printed guidebook=

The area is a disused quarry. The crag is south-facing and suitable for climbing from early spring until late autumn. The walls are around 10–15 m high and consist largely of quarried but solid rock. Everything is bolted, with a few exceptions that are top-rope routes.

body.text: The area is a disused quarry. The crag is sunny and suitable for climbing from early spring to late autumn. The walls are approximately 10–15m high and consist largely of quarried but solid rock. Everything is bolted, with a few top-rope-only exceptions.
No problems.

Drive to Grötvik. Continue past the marina and café; the quarry appears on the right after a few hundred metres. There are several older bolted routes on the left-hand side when viewed from the sea. The new routes are on the wall straight ahead.

Do not turn up towards the first crag you see; the routes are a little further on.

A classic trad crag 90 km west of Umeå. Stora väggen is around 60 m high and has many quality routes, often following a crack from bottom to top. Many routes have a steeper first pitch followed by a less steep, slightly easier second pitch. Lilla väggen is around 40 m high and steeper, with most routes vertical or slightly overhanging.

As most pitches are around 30 metres long, a larger rack than a standard Swedish trad rack is needed; if you place one piece every two metres and use two pieces at the belay, you will need 16 pieces per pitch. Climbers who are good at finding nut placements can safely climb many routes with a double set of nuts and just a few cams or hexes. Nowadays, however, most people use a standard rack consisting of one set of nuts, one set of small cams from fingertip to finger size, and one and a half to two sets of cams from finger to hand-jam size, with an extra fist-sized cam.

A comparison of different cams in PDF format (http://www.sverigeforaren.se/images/0/02/Tabell-kamkilar.pdf).

From the coast, take road 92 via Bjurholm. After Balsjö there is an airstrip-like straight. After this and another long straight, turn left towards Ö Nyland, 2 km. In the village, turn left again towards Bredträsk. Drive for about 1 km and continue straight ahead at the next junction, where a road turns off to the right towards Bredträsk. After a short distance, turn left onto a small forest road.

Keep left at the Y-junction after a couple of kilometres.

Blåberget is about 4 km from the turn onto the forest road. When the crag comes into view, you should also be able to see a small parking area. A clear path begins by the parking area, with steps leading up towards the crag. There is also a small stream here with drinking water. On the way up to the crag, the path passes a level area with a shelter, fire pit, outdoor toilet and tent pitches.

To reach Lilla väggen, follow the signs, cross the bridge and then follow the blue trail markings. About 100 metres after the bridge, a path appears on the right. Take it and follow it uphill for 40 metres, then turn left onto a faint path. See also the map below.

The approach from the south is drier and preferable during May. A few kilometres after Bjurholm on road 92, turn towards Karlsbäck. After crossing the Lögdeälven river in the village of Nedre Nyland, approximately 13 km after leaving road 92, take the first right towards Abborrtjärn. Drive for just under a kilometre, then turn off where the first forest road branches right. The forest road has a small sign pointing towards Slottestjärn. Continue straight ahead where the road branches towards the tarn. Keep right at the next Y-junction, and Lilla väggen at Blåberget will soon come into view.
Available from Umeå Klätter- och Högfjällsklubb (http://www.ukhk.se) or Utebutiken in Umeå (http://utebutiken.se/).

To reach Lilla väggen, follow the signs and cross the bridge. About 100 metres after the bridge, a faint path appears on the right. Take it and follow it for 40 metres, then turn left onto an even fainter path. Follow the blue trail markings. See also the map above.
There are quite a few winter and aid routes that have never been properly documented. Perhaps now is the time?
So far, no routes have been rebolted, so the bolts were probably installed in the year of the first ascent (see Umeföraren) or in the preceding few years. The following applies to additional bolting:

Abseil line above Ikaros. The upper anchor is completely new. The anchor at the first belay received one new bolt; the other bolt is older.

Upper abseil anchor on the abseil line starting at the top of Steneld.

A popular two-pitch trad crag 90 km west of Umeå. Stora väggen (the Great Wall) is around 60 m high and ranges from vertical to slabby. Lilla väggen (the Small Wall) is around 35 m high and vertical to slightly overhanging.

The ‘Nya leder’ section describes routes established since the guidebook was printed. The ‘Rekommenderade leder’ section describes the best routes at Blåberget. ‘Vinterleder och tekniska turer’ describes mixed winter routes and aid routes. There is also quite a lot of waterfall ice on Lilla väggen.

From Umeå, take the E12 west towards Vännäs. After passing Vännäsby, take road 92 towards Bjurholm/Fredrika. After passing Balsjö, there are two long straights. A short distance after the second, a road sign points left towards Ö Nyland. Follow this road to the tiny village, then turn left towards Bredträsk. Continue for 1 km to the first junction. Turn left and continue for 200–300 metres until you see a small logging road on the left. Follow it for 4 km until the crag appears on the right. There is ample parking on both sides of the road. From the parking area, follow a well-worn path up to the crag.

It is possible to climb at Stora väggen from mid-April, but the final 4 km of road is not open during winter. The last section is normally driveable after early May, though it can be rough going on spring afternoons. The change from rock-climbing to mixed conditions generally occurs around late October or early November.

The routes are quite long, with both pitches around 30 m (110 ft), so a large rack is advisable. A minimalist rack should include at least one set of nuts, with doubles in the mid-sizes, and a set of cams from 0.75 inches to 3.5 inches (Friend #1 through #4, or Camalot #.5 to #3.5). Small cams are useful on the harder routes. Bear in mind that most belays are not equipped with bolts.

Generally speaking, Blåberget has very solid rock, but beware of rocks on ledges and at the tops of the crags. Take care not to dislodge any rocks when belaying from the top of the crag!

There is little or no mobile phone reception down at the parking area, but reception is good at the main crag.

The letters a–e after the difficulty grade in the route descriptions above refer to the ‘gruesomeness grade’.

The second pitch has now been climbed entirely free. From the top of the first pitch, walk straight across the ledge and locate an overhanging corner with two bolts. Climb the corner, then a slab to a ledge (7). A very hard boulder problem (Fb 7A+), or pulling on two pieces of protection (one bolt and one nut: Wallnut #1 or similar), leads to the top. The knife-blade pitons were placed in the early 1990s and found to be in good condition when checked in 2008, but that is no guarantee they will hold...

The island of Branthäll lies in the Söderhamn archipelago. A ten-metre sea cliff.

body.text: The island of Branthäll lies in the Söderhamn archipelago. It has a ten-metre-high sea cliff.

By boat: If you have your own boat, or can borrow one, reaching the island presents no great difficulty, but it is also possible without one. During the summer, you can take the tourist boat "Sunnan" and be dropped off on the island, then collected on the final trip of the day or the following day. "Sunnan" departs from the quay opposite Söderhamn railway station. GPS coordinates: Lat: N 61º 15' 40.43" Long: E 17º 13' 8.36"

This south-facing wall is up to ten metres high and approximately 75 m long. The routes start at water level or from rocks in the water. The climbing follows cracks in exceptionally clean, excellent granite. The most interesting section is the right-hand half of the wall. The routes are generally top-roped, with the climber lowered on a Sticht plate.

body.text: Bodberget is about 40 km from Söderhamn and 7 kilometres from Arbrå. It is the right-hand one of the three hills east of the village of Flästa. Norrberget is nearby, as are Hisnopp and Torpberget.
Birds nest at Bodberget, so climbing is prohibited until 1 August.

Travelling by car is the most practical option. From Söderhamn, take county road 301 towards Bollnäs. After about 20 kilometres, you reach the village of Glössbo; turn right at a sign reading "ARBRÅ 27". After about 18 km, you reach the village of Flästa. Turn right here at a sign reading "ÖJUNGEN 6". After one and a half kilometres, you are directly below Bodberget. Parking is available 100 metres beyond the second military storehouse along the road. From here, follow an old forestry vehicle track uphill into the woods. Follow the waymarkers to the crag.

It is also possible to travel by bus. From Bollnäs, take a bus to Arbrå, then change to a bus for Flästa. Get off where it turns around, at the far end of Flästa by the "ÖJUNGEN 6" sign, then walk the remaining distance (just over one and a half km).

GPS coordinates for the crag: Lat: N 61º 26' 47.15" Long: E 16º 30' 56.38"

The walls face south. At the far right is Östpelaren, with two large, obvious overhangs. To its left is the main wall, shaped like a large amphitheatre carved out of the rock. It is approximately 100 m wide and 50 m high, but has a rather broken appearance. To the left of the main wall is a distinct rock ridge, a buttress rising towards the top of the hill at an angle of about 60 degrees, known as Kvicksilverpelaren. To the left of the buttress is a loose gully that was formerly used as a descent route. To the left of the gully is the so-called practice crag. It is about 75 m wide and 20 m high and, at least in the past when the crag was more frequently visited, was almost entirely brushed clean of lichen and other unpleasantness. The descent is straightforward and goes through the woods to the left of the practice crag.

description.text: (by the descent) 75 m up the descent from the cairn at the start of "Endast för galningar" is a wide fist crack.
30 m up the descent from the cairn. Starts on an ill-defined pillar, then continues onto a five-metre slab. Finishes with a hand traverse left along the top of the slab. Unprotected.
Starts behind the pine about 10 m left of the cairn, at a diagonal crack by some protruding blocks. About 4 m up, step right over a bulge onto a ledge, then follow cracks straight up to the top.

FFA: Kent Andersson, Per Calleberg and Pär Nolgård, 1981. Starts on the wall just right of the previous route. Follow thin cracks diagonally up right to a red corner, crossing it to the right with a bridging move. Then follow the obvious crack straight up the wall to a pine.
Starts in a small corner right of the previous route. Follow the red corner formation to the top.

A rather wandering and contrived line. Starts right of "Änglavakt" by stepping onto it. About 6 m up, make a short traverse right to an alcove. From there, follow the right-hand of two parallel cracks diagonally up left. Then follow a rotten corner to the top.

FFA: Mikael Bäckman, Anders Dahl and Kent Andersson, 1980. Starts on the smooth slab opposite the cairn. Climb straight up to the alcove, then right to a ledge. Continue right onto the wall, then straight up.

Starts in the corner behind the pine right of the cairn. Climb diagonally up left to the alcove. Continue straight up the left-hand of the two diagonal cracks. The crack leads to a ledge; from there, climb straight up to the top. 2 bolts.
From the ledge on "Endast för galningar", climb diagonally up left to bolts, then straight up past a bolt through small roofs until joining the finishing crack of "Le Freak", which is followed.

FFA: Kent Andersson, 1983. Same start as Vårled, then straight up over a small overhang. Climb past a bolt and then up the obvious corner. Bear left and follow a diagonal crack to the top. Start in the same corner as "Vårled", but move right across the ledge to reach the large, obvious flake. Follow the edge of the flake and stand up on the ledge above. Continue diagonally up right past bolts.

FFA: Per Calleberg and Kent Andersson, 1982. Start in the same corner as "Vårled", but move right across the ledge to reach the large, obvious flake. Follow the edge of the flake and stand up on the ledge above. Continue diagonally up right past bolts.

FFA: Per Calleberg and Pär Nolgård, 1983. Follow the groove on the wall a few metres left of "Hybris". On reaching the flake leading up to "The Bisquit", step out right and follow "Hybris" for a couple of metres. As soon as possible, step back left and climb straight up the steep wall.
Start in a short corner just over five metres right of the cairn. Continue over ledges and up the broken band of red rock beneath the obvious sloping slab.
A finish variation to "Hybris". Goes straight out right along the obvious crack in the large sloping slab.
Climb a short wall onto a low-angled slab. Climb to the birch and follow the obvious gully separating the practice wall from the prominent slab on the right. Dirty and not recommended.

FFA: Per Calleberg and Kent Andersson, 1982. Climb a short wall. Angle up across the slab to a birch. Follow the grooves along the left edge of the slab, past a bolt, and continue to the top past another bolt.

FFA: Buster Delin and Kent Andersson, 1984. Climb the slab to the base of the main slab. Follow a series of irregularities straight up its centre. The bolt on "Tilltaget" can usefully be clipped for protection. Unfortunately, it tends to become overgrown quickly.
FFA: Buster Delin, 1984. Climb the slab to the base of the main slab. Follow a series of irregularities along its right edge. Unprotected.
Starts in the hollow left of the loose gully. Follow a corner formation to a slab, then make a diagonal traverse right.
Between Övningsklippan and Kvicksilverpelaren is a loose gully (Det ska vi fira) formerly used as a descent. It is loose, however, and should be avoided.

Follows the left edge of Kvicksilverpelaren. After about one pitch, move right onto the pillar itself and continue straight up to the top of the crag. Loose. Should be avoided, at least in summer, due to the risk of rockfall.

Friction climbing along the top of Kvicksilverpelaren. One pitch, 2 bolts. Belay on the right by a large pine. Easy downclimbing to the left, around the corner. Eventually this reaches "Det skall vi fira", which is not particularly loose this far down.
Wall climbing three metres left of "Take Off". Joins "Take Off" after about 10 metres. 2 or 3 bolts.

25 m up the right-hand side of Kvicksilverpelaren is an obvious corner formation capped by an overhang. Climb the corner, traverse left beneath the roof, then follow the gully to the belay pine on "Över noll". Variation: pass the roof on its right-hand side.
FFA: Kent Andersson and Torbjörn Nilsson, 1982. Climbs the cracked wall immediately right of "Take Off". Continue onto the slab above. 1 bolt on the slab. Belay as for "Över noll".

FFA: Kent Andersson, Torbjörn Nilsson and Åke Englund, 1983. Start as for "Stridspiloten", but bear right beneath a small roof. Step up into a diagonal crack and follow it diagonally up right to the top. To extend the climbing, move onto the final metres of "Stridspiloten" after reaching a ledge above the diagonal crack. Belay as for "Över noll".
Starts 7 metres right of "Venuspassagen". Follow the crack to 2 bolts placed side by side. From here, a shallow and somewhat poorly protected corner leads straight up (crux).

Start as for "Stridspiloten", but soon traverse right and follow "Venuspassagen" for a few metres. Then continue the rising traverse for a full pitch. Belay at a large flake directly below and slightly to one side of a large sloping overhang. Poorly protected in places.
Fine flake climbing. This route takes the wall left of "Den onde". Climb the obvious flakes and finish up a crack.

Free ascent: Torbjörn Nilsson and Anders Byfält, 1983. The obvious crackless corner formation about 75 m uphill from the start of "Take Off". At the left edge of the sloping overhang is a V-shaped recess. To reach it, climb "Den gode" for a metre or so, then make a huge step across into the V. After climbing the V, traverse left for a metre or so, step over a bulge onto a slab and climb it. Protected by one bolt. The second must not unclip the rope from the bolt before becoming established in the V!
Free ascent: Torbjörn Nilsson and Buster Delin, 1983. From the start of "Den onde", move right across a slab beneath a sloping overhang, step out onto an arete and continue.

The route follows the chimney at the junction of the main face and the pillar to its left, and has a distinctly mountaineering character. Climb a short chimney behind a rock slab. Continue up a corner and climb the chimney itself, "Skithålet". On emerging from the chimney, move left along two ledges and climb a short crack to a pine. Belay. Go right behind a large block, then straight up a gully to the top.

Starts in an obvious black streak between "Skitleden" and the large overhang crossed by "Överheten". Step left, then continue straight up. Somewhat inconsistent climbing, protected by a couple of fixed pegs but otherwise not much else.

Take the easiest possible line to the start of the corner on "Genesis". Traverse left for ten metres, then climb straight up beneath the large roof. (Alternatively, climb the corner on Genesis, then make a skyhook traverse left directly beneath the roof.) The aid climbing begins at a fixed peg and becomes increasingly awkward higher up. Eventually gain ledges covered in lingonberry scrub, then traverse a long way left to a pine belay.

This route follows the large, obvious corner in the middle of the main face. Climb an overhanging recess in white rock to a birch beneath an overhang. Move left and up, then climb the corner to a large ledge. Continue up and right to a large ledge with a large pine and large blocks. If you search in a crack here, you may still find a tin containing a visitors’ book. If so, sign it! From here, follow the gully beginning at the left edge of the ledge to the top.

Traverse left beneath the prominent roof to the left of the finish of "Genesis". Here you will be standing astride an unpleasant rock flake, so a fall from this point could have very unfortunate consequences. Then climb straight up the face above. Bear in mind that a size 1½ Friend may be useful.
Not much of a route in its own right, but used to vary the finishes of "Genesis" and "Anabola stereoider".
From the middle of "Traverstvärs", a short jamming crack leads up to the right edge of a large roof. Climb it, then traverse left to the finish of "Genesis".

At the left edge of the obvious red face, a thin crack runs diagonally up left past a prominent flake to the right edge of a small ledge. Follow the cracks straight up to the large pine at the "Genesis" belay. Strenuous at the start.

Start as for "Stjärnornas Krog", but at the small ledge move left and round a corner into a corner. Climb straight up to an overhang. Step diagonally up right, then continue straight up a gully to the "Genesis" belay.
Follow "Rymdimperiet Slår tillbaka" to the overhang. Traverse left for a short distance, then climb the crack above the overhang. Continue straight up to the "Genesis" belay.

Start from a ledge below an obvious red face. Climb straight up the face, just left of a left-facing corner, until it begins to overhang. Step right round the corner and make a difficult move onto a narrow edge. Move slightly right, then climb straight up to a small ledge with a small rowan. Continue straight up the wide crack to a rounded ledge. Belay. From here, either climb straight to the top over somewhat loose, poorly protected rock, or traverse left to the finish of "Genesis".

Free ascent: Mikael Bäckman, 1986. A variation to "Anabola Steroider". Continue straight up where it begins to overhang, following the left edge of the rock slab to its top. Then traverse right onto "Anabola Steroider".

There is a bolt on the face to the right of the starting corner of "Anabola Steroider". Climb to it, then make a short traverse right to a peg. From here, climb straight up to join "Anabola Steroider".

A face route consisting of two short sections separated by a ledge. It finishes just left of the start of "Rock & Roll". The route is actually on a smaller section of face below the main wall, which tends to become overgrown quickly. It can now hardly be climbed without a thorough clean, if anyone can even find it any more... 2 pegs.

Start as for "Rock & Roll" and climb nearly all of it. Then branch left and follow a system of corners to a ledge. Climb through an overhang and, after a short traverse left, follow a crack straight to the top. Exposed. 2 pegs.
Start at the foot of "Bäckfallet", but climb up left and follow the obvious corner. The route joins "Bäckfallet" above the large ledge halfway up.

Follows the corner at the right edge of the face, to the right of a prominent large “nose”. Climb straight up the obvious corner to a large ledge on top of the “nose”. (Possible belay.) Continue up the corner above, finishing with a short layback before exiting right.
A low-angled, now forgotten face route in the ravine behind Bodberget’s right-hand pillar. 2 pegs.
A compact corner on the east side of the largest block on the scree slope below the main face.

Climb for a metre or so up a crack immediately right of "Bäckfallet". Traverse right to a peg. Climb up, then diagonally left past another peg. Climb straight up to the left of the bolt (crux) until a good hold is reached at the edge of a recess. Step into the recess, but immediately move back out left and then up onto the ledge.
Start as for "Den Muterade Hackspetten", but climb straight up from the first peg to the recess with the pine. 1 bolt and 2 pegs.

Starts left of "Krakel Spektakel". Climb easy ground to a ledge beneath the large roof. Continue to the obvious jamming crack. Just below the roof, move left past a peg. Eventually descend to the start of "Bäckfallet". Belay. The second pitch initially follows a crack parallel to "Bäckfallet", then branches right to reach a large ledge. From here, climb the wide diagonal crack to the top.

Bodberget’s old classic, desperate top-rope problem was eventually climbed on lead. Thin face climbing on the wall left of Kusin vitamin. It starts hard and only becomes more vicious higher up. 2 bolts. The first ascensionist pre-placed a nut at the finish while inspecting the route on abseil.
At the left-hand end of the large ledge where "Krakel Spektakel" finishes, climb over a few blocks to reach two thin, parallel cracks trending diagonally right. Follow the wider crack to the top.

Climb easy rock up the large corner on the right-hand pillar. Belay on the ledge beneath the large roof. Follow the crack straight out beneath the roof (large nuts), then climb straight up past the roof when possible. Continue up an easy groove to a large ledge.
Follow the overhanging diagonal crack line on the wall right of "Krakel Spektakel". Then move left up a short slab before climbing the overhanging corner above.

One classic problem, some lines still to be done, and a few more good problems with quick access from Brommaplan. Not everything is fully documented, and some problems may need a session with a wire brush, but it is well worth an evening.

=Directions=
There is a small long-stay car park behind the OK petrol station at Brommaplan. Park there and head into the woods on the other side of the footpath. Follow the path for just under 100 m; the first wall appears on the right.

=Topo=

=Problems=
(A) The wall
(B) Boulder 1
(C) Boulder 2
There are one or more undocumented problems here.
(D) Boulder 3
A short distance further on, past boulder (C).
Very low start.
Right edge and crack are out.
Follow the crack, topping out right of the cleft.
Straight up.
Straight up on good holds.
Start with left hand on the undercling and right hand on the crimp. Climb the arete.
Sit start on the jug, then head slightly right into Kennethklubben.
The exact line is somewhat uncertain.
Start on two sidepulls.
Sit start on the rail, move right and top out left.
Traverse from the corner and top out as for Grothens Escape.
Straight up without using the edges of the block.
Warm-up.
Follow the edge straight up without branching off.
Straight up the crack.
Start on the lowest holds in the hollow, with lots of eliminates.
Fridge-hug diagonally up right; the ledge on the left is out.
0 documented routes and problems.

There are three walls of interest: an overhanging section in a shady, mosquito-ridden spot; a good section in an ‘amphitheatre’ by a barbecue area; and finally a small slab. The problems have been developed by various people over the years.

body.text: There are three walls of interest: a shady, mosquito-ridden overhanging section; a good section in an ‘amphitheatre’ by a barbecue area; and finally a small slab. The problems have been developed by various people over the years.

Take metro green line 17 towards Skarpnäck and get off at either Bagarmossen or Skarpnäck.

Make your way to Brotorpstugan on the edge of Nackareservatet, between Bagarmossen and Skarpnäck. If travelling by car, you can park at the end of Klarälvsvägen, provided you park considerately. Then follow the footpath straight into Nackareservatet and down towards Brotorpstugan.

From Brotorpstugan, follow the gravel road towards Älta, crossing the stream and continuing straight ahead. After about 500 metres, the road crosses a marshy area with a drainage pipe running beneath it. Go back 20 metres and take the path on the left when facing towards Älta. Follow the path for 30 metres to reach the overhanging wall by a deer feeding station. Continue along the path for another 50–100 metres, heading uphill to the right. You are now at Amfiteatern, with a large barbecue area in the middle.

To reach the slab, turn right instead at the overhanging wall and head uphill a short way along the edge.
Old guide: Klaan (http://www.klaan.nu/brotorp/area.html#)
From left to right...
From left to right...
TODO

description.text: Sit start left of 'inget' on good holds, then straight up into the crack and top out right via 'inget'.
Sit start on good holds, then climb up and right and top out.
Sit start with LH on an edge and RH in the crack. Follow the crack to the jug, then continue straight up and top out.

Sit start directly behind the two large trees on a large flat hold. Finishes after three moves at a large jug; this would have been the top if the tree were not there, as it would otherwise be possible to top out.
About 2 metres right of the two large trees. Sit start with LH on an edge and RH on a better edge. Take the easiest line up: three moves to the large rounded hold, match, then top out.

About 2 metres right of the two large trees. A variant of "Al Taneen" that avoids the large rounded hold and instead climbs more directly up to the left. The rounded hold is out for both hands and feet.
Standing start with both hands on the jug, then climb to an edge and continue up left. Note: the good footholds and handholds to the left are out. Green line (sit-start project, yellow).
Sit start with LH in an undercut/sidepull and RH in a pocket. Traverse left and top out. Red line.
Sit start with LH on a good edge and RH on a good flake. Catch a good hold above, then continue straight up and top out. Blue line.
Hanging start with both hands on the jug. Traverse left and finish up 'Det är vad det är'. Black(?) line.

A handful of crags along the shore. The climbing is mainly mixed, requiring both your own wires and protection bolts. Sundsvalls klätterklubb has been gradually re-equipping the crag for some time, and it now also offers some excellent sport and trad routes. Brattberget has around 60 routes of varying quality.

body.text: A handful of crags on the shore. The climbing is mainly mixed, requiring both your own nuts and supplementary bolts. Sundsvalls klätterklubb has been gradually refurbishing the crag for some time, and it now also offers some excellent sport and trad routes. Brattberget has around 60 routes of varying quality.

Brattberget was developed in the days when men were men and climbers were even more so. Several routes were established using marginal homemade protection, and by modern standards they cannot be described as particularly well protected.

As a result, many good but lethal routes in the grade 5–6 range have become covered in moss. Sundsvalls klätterklubb has therefore begun carefully rebolting the crag to make some of these dormant routes accessible to a wider audience. Brattberget now offers a number of excellent fully bolted sport routes and trad routes, while the old classics have received—and continue to receive—new anchors.

Some routes at Brattberget are mixed, requiring a few nuts before the rest is bolted. Or vice versa. Many routes have top anchors or nearby anchors—including trad classics such as Skrubben—which can be accessed from above. Bring a few long slings if you want to set up a top-rope.

Most holds are crimps, with the occasional crack. The walls are just off vertical.

From Sundsvall, drive south for around 15 km to Kvissleby. At the traffic lights, turn left towards Essvik. If approaching from the south on the E4, pass Kvissleby centre on your right and turn right at the traffic lights.

Cross the Ljungan and turn left immediately after the bridge. Drive through Essvik and Stockstrand, then continue until you reach some storage tanks. A couple of hundred metres beyond the tanks, a short road turns off to the left. Take this road and park in the grove. Follow the path over the hill to the shelter—just over a five-minute approach. The crag itself lies south-east of the shelter, or to its right when approaching from the car park.

There are two ways down to the crag, apart from abseiling. Either:

• Head fairly directly towards the water, diagonally down and right from the shelter. This leads to a large, bouldery descent gully that can be scrambled down with some care. Or,
• Follow the path along the top of the crag to the far end of Stora väggen. Fixed ropes help you down the path to the start of Over the top. This approach is recommended for first-time visitors.
East-facing and close to the sea. As a result, the season starts fairly late here, but in midsummer it is wonderfully cool and pleasant. If it is still too warm, you can take a dip between routes.

There are good options for staying overnight by the shelter at the top of the hill. Comfortable tent pitches can be difficult to find, so bring a mosquito net and sleep in the shelter.

There is also a toilet near the shelter. You need to bring your own water.

The routes at Brattberget are spread across six walls. If you want to abseil in, there are good anchors on virtually every wall, with the possible exception of Vikingaväggen, which is slightly difficult to access from above.

The routes below are listed from left to right. Several routes, particularly on Stora väggen, have a stone at the start bearing the route name. Pure sport routes are given French grades, while trad and mixed routes use Swedish grades.

Stora väggen lies above all the other crags and is the only one not directly beside the shore. The easiest way to reach it is to follow the path along the top of the hill, then descend the path leading to Over the top. From there, it is easy to continue down to the other crags on the shore.
This is the short left-hand side of Sjöväggen.
Radioväggen lies below Stora väggen and is a good crag for setting up top-ropes for children or beginners. You need nuts and slings to build the anchors.
A printer-friendly version of the guide can be found on LKK’s website (http://www.linkopingsklatterklubb.se/index.php/forare_sverige.html).

description.text: Rebolted in 2008. Now has more bolts than before and a new anchor.
Excellent face climbing. The route has new bolts and is now fully bolted. Lower-off.
Initially follows the flake crack, followed by delicate face climbing. Good. Rebolted in 2008 and now fully bolted. Anchor.
An overhang at the start, followed by a crack. Poorly protected.
Follows a prominent crack. Bolted anchor.
Starts in the corner left of Berkaleden, then finishes slightly left towards the dark hole.
Starts right of the arete. A good mixed route.
Follows flake features just right of Berkaleden. Anchor.
Follows a crack system. Poorly protected near the top.
Belay from a single glue-in bolt or continue to Vängåvan ledge. Additional bolts added in 2009.
Follows a prominent crack. Bolted anchor.
Fully bolted. Anchor.
Abseil from Vängåvan.
Starts in the corner and follows the crack upwards. Anchor.
Sustained face climbing. Good. Anchor.
Sustained face climbing. Good. Anchor.
Follow the arete to the "tooth", then go straight over the overhang; it feels slightly exposed. You can escape halfway up to the anchor of "Inre röster", making it 6a. Anchor. Well protected.

Starts behind "Prokrastinering", taking the chimney and the crack above the arete. Where the crack bends left, join "Inre röster" and continue to its anchor. Alternatively, finish via "Prokrastinering" using its bolt. Bolted anchor.
Follows a prominent crack to an anchor halfway up the wall. A standard rack up to Camalot #3 works, though a large cam such as a Camalot #4 is useful. Bolted anchor.
Follows the corner and crack lines all the way up. Bolted anchor.
Sustained face climbing. Good. Anchor.
Follows a crack for some distance before finishing in a corner-like feature. Brushed and cleaned in 2009; the previously unprotected finish now has an additional bolt.
The bolts are widely spaced.
Cleaned and brushed in 2012.
Poorly protected at the crux.
Shares the first three bolts with Ont i stjärten, then continues straight up through the roof. A few cams can be placed before the roof.
Fully bolted since 2008. Well protected.
Poorly protected, loose and nasty.
Follow the crack slightly right of Trappstegen.
The crack in the roof.
The left-hand of the two prominent right-leaning cracks. A distinct crux a few metres up. A Camalot #4 or larger is required to protect the crux adequately.
The right-hand of the two prominent right-leaning cracks.
The chimney section is straightforward and not awkward.

Go beneath the large block and scramble up to a ledge, where there is an off-width crack on the left. Unusual and interesting climbing with several possible variants. Two are described below. At least two large cams such as Camalot #4s are useful for comfortable protection.
Find the easiest line near the crack.
The chockstones are out of bounds and the route must finish via the crack. Slightly awkward.
Take the wide crack up to the large chockstone. Go around the arete into the corner, then straight up.
Climb the crack. On reaching Mindre vibrationer, follow the flake left. Anchor.
Straight up over the bulge. Shares its first bolt with Mindre vibrationer. There is a fair distance from the first bolt to the next protection. Anchor.
Starts in a prominent crack. After the first bolt, the route trends diagonally left to the cracks left of the overhang, then continues straight up.
Follows the arete.
Follows the arete.
The route follows a "Z" pattern.
The crag classic. Follows a crack that is very wide at first and gradually narrows. Anchor.
Sustained sport climbing. Bolted anchor installed in 2008.
Short. Anchor.
You do not want to experience Neros pendel. Brushed in 2012.
Fractured rock with good holds.
After the first bolt, climb round the arete and clip a bolt round the corner. Then climb back round the arete.
Hard to protect near the top.
Follow the corner from start to finish.
Same start as Premiärdiedern. New anchor and additional bolts added in 2009.
Mantle up and belay at the anchor. Cleaned in 2012.
Slab route just beyond the descent. Belay from a single bolt.
Bolted corner at the end of the descent.
Short route over two blocks in the descent crack.
Starts on the large boulder. Goes round the corner, then straight up.
Follows a crack system, traversing left about 3 metres up. Well protected.
Shares the start with Nya chanser. Continue straight up where Nya chanser traverses left.
Starts to the right of Stela chanser and shares its finish.
A fully bolted sport route, completed in 2009. Two-bolt anchor.
Loose.
Cam placement at the top.
Loose.

=Brunnberget= (also Ramelsberget on the map) A crag visible from Route 40 through Ulricehamn. Said to have some fine trad, around 20 m high. The longest route is 30 m. 2010-04-21: NOTE!!! Sensitive nesting site; climbing prohibited up to and including 15/7. Disturbance has been observed and the area is being monitored. /Johan Svensson, access officer for Bohusläns KK. Unfortunately, the crag is often wet, but the climbing is fun, with good holds on the highly featured gneiss. Try fucking hard 8+9-, perhaps the hardest route east of Gothenburg. Add more if you know the area! Is this map correct?

description.text: =General= Sundsvall’s largest bouldering area. =Directions= Take the E4 south from Sundsvall towards Njurundabommen. Turn left by the church ruins/ICA Bommen (approx. 18 min from Sundsvall). Continue straight ahead for about 500 m, then turn left at the sign for Björkön/Bergafjärden. Follow the road straight ahead, passing beneath a viaduct and over a bridge. Then turn left and continue straight ahead for about 200 m. Follow the signs for Lörudden/Björkön and continue for 3 km, then turn towards Bergafjärden and follow the tarmac road for ~1 km. Take the upper-right road at the five-way junction (in practice, just follow the tarmac road). Take the first left, by a blue postbox. At the end of the road, turn left onto a small forest track. Park in one of the pull-ins after 50–100 m. Follow the first path you passed on the right before parking for about 500 m until the first boulder comes into view. Along the way, you will cross a meadow with a red cottage. When you see the first boulder straight ahead in the power-line clearing, the crag is on your left.

body.text:
=Sectors=

A PDF guide by Fredrik Bodin is available here: http://sundsvallsklatterklubb.se/index.php?page=forare
Head out onto the field, then take the first path on the right. This is the first boulder you reach, after about 100 metres.
The second boulder you come to after leaving the field and passing the first boulder.
Down to the right of the path when you have "En annan sten" up to the left. A very pleasant boulder with some easier problems up to 6A and good landings.

description.text: Starts high on the boulder and goes straight up via the hole.
Starts where the large block has broken away and goes straight up.
Start established with hands on the large undercling; straight up.
Straight up without standing on the boulder below. No jump start.
Start established with both hands on the low sloper; straight up.
Starts where the ground is lowest, left of the arete, and goes straight up. Morpho.
Starts with hands on the small arete and goes straight up. No jump start.

Brunna (the crag has long been known locally by this name) consists of a north-facing wall and a west-facing wall. Large meadows lie below these 30-metre walls. All routes in the grade 5–6 range are protected with nuts.

body.text: Brunna (the crag has long been known locally by this name) consists of a north-facing wall and a west-facing wall. Large meadows lie below these 30-metre walls. All routes in the grade 5–6 range are protected with nuts.

Mörkrets hjärta (the crag has no local name) was discovered by Jappe Pålsgård while out running a few years ago. On his way back, he noticed a crag on the other side of a large meadow – "Brunna". The crags lie between Hallinden and Brodalen.

Anders at Staxängs gård owns the land below both walls, the tractor track with a small parking bay, and the Mörkrets hjärta crag itself. Brunna is owned by Jon at Tvetens gård. A slightly larger parking area – providing access to both crags (P2 in the next picture) – is owned by Jan-Åke at Kanekärr. Jappe is on good terms with Anders, who is happy for people to climb on his land and also to park at P1 (though not in winter!). Jan Leyon is also in contact with Anders. Jan is on good terms with Jan-Åke, who has no objection to people parking on his land. Jan has met Jon, who has no objection to us climbing at Brunna.

Take road 162 from Hallinden towards Lysekil.

For parking at P1:

After about a kilometre, take the road on the left opposite the "Staxäng" bus stop. Turn 90 degrees right, then after a short distance 90 degrees left, and continue uphill. Halfway up, there is a tractor track on the left. Turn onto it and park.

For parking at P2:

Continue for another 700 metres past the "Staxäng" bus stop. Turn left and keep right when the road eventually forks. After passing the summer cottage on your left, you will see Jan-Åke’s house ahead on the left, behind some outbuildings. Cross the meadow east of Jan-Åke’s house and pass through a short stretch of flat woodland. This brings you to the large meadow below Brunna.

Easy ascent/descent to the left of the North Wall. Three routes have been established on the wall:

description.text: Far left of the wall. Start in a large corner and climb straight up the obvious crack. Pine tree at the top-out. Sustained at the grade.

Far right of the wall. Start in a hand crack beneath two blocks. Traverse left, then climb the crack and continue up crack systems to a pine tree at the top. The start is almost the crux of the route – and the traverse is not just a walk...

At the far left of the wall. Start in the obvious left-facing corner. The crux comes almost immediately, with just enough gear to keep it enjoyable. Your reward is then 25 metres of superb climbing on slightly larger features!

Pitch 1. (Grade 6) Starts 50 m left of "En tjur och nio kvigor". Climb flakes to the obvious crack/weakness through the large roof. Pass this and continue up flakes and cracks to a large ledge. Pitch 2. (Grade 6/5-) Either continue directly up rightwards in the corner (grade 5-, dirty), or move a few metres left and climb the obvious crack system. (Grade 6)

Far right of the wall. Start behind a large block in the left-facing corner. The route is initially overhanging, but easier than it looks here. The angle then eases and the difficulty increases. Abseil sling with a maillon on a pine tree 10 metres right of the top-out.

10 metres right of the previous route. Scramble up to a ledge with two trees, 5 metres above the ground. Climb the chimney to the overhang. Initially use both cracks, then top out via the left-hand one. Good hand jams at the crux! Abseil sling with a maillon on a pine tree 5 metres above the top-out.
0 documented routes and problems.

The crag lies within the Huskvarnabergen Nature Reserve, which forms part of the Östra Vätterstranden area of national interest. It covers a 3.5-kilometre stretch of the fault escarpment overlooking Vättern and the plateau to the east, whose highest parts rise to over 300 metres above sea level. The rock consists of lime-rich greenstones (diorite and gabbro) that weather easily, resulting in many good cracks. In places, however, the rock is quite smooth, offering little friction.

body.text: The crag lies in the Huskvarnabergen Nature Reserve, which forms part of the Östra Vätterstranden area of national interest. It occupies a 3.5-kilometre stretch of the fault scarp overlooking Vättern and the plateau to the east, whose highest parts are over 300 metres above sea level. The rock consists of calcium-rich greenstones (diorite and gabbro) that weather easily, resulting in many fine cracks. In places, however, the rock is fairly smooth, providing little friction.

The right of public access does not apply in full within the Huskvarnabergen Nature Reserve. Among other things, you may not camp; pick liverleaf, yellow anemone, lily of the valley, globeflower or cowslip; dig up shrubs, herbs or ferns; or remove mosses, lichens or tree-growing fungi. Dogs and other pets must not be allowed to roam freely. Fires may only be lit in specially designated places. Signs, notices, inscriptions, orienteering controls, posters or similar installations must not be permanently erected. No bolting is permitted.
Brunnstoprs café can be a good place to start or finish a visit. It is open until 23.00 on most days from June to August. During May and September, it is open at weekends when the weather is fine.

description.text: A small, unassuming crag 5 km south-west of Bålsta. The crag is up to 8 m high and about 20 m wide.

body.text: A small, inconspicuous crag 5 km south-west of Bålsta. The crag is up to 8 m high and approximately 20 m wide.

PLEASE NOTE: Access to the crag is restricted. Entry onto rock faces and cliffs is prohibited from 1 January to 31 July.

A guide with topo is available at Stockholms Nätförare (http://www.plonk.se/snf/bryggviken.html), Bryggviken (http://www.plonk.se/oldsite/bryggviken.html)

description.text: Obvious left-leaning corner starting with a prominent roof. The roof is passed using an old piton with a sling. The slippery corner is protected with somewhat hard-to-find but good nuts and cams. An old/rotten bolt on the headwall.

The free version of Farenheit 450. Pass the roof on the left with a few well-directed pulls, then make a short traverse into the corner. Finish via an obvious finger crack about 1.5 metres right of the bolt. Well protected, with lots of small cams.
The parallel line to the next route. Lower-off anchor.

The most obvious left-leaning crack in the middle of the wall. Lower-off anchor slightly to the left at the top. A variation climbs half of Grymt Slut before continuing into Dagstur. This is called Snällt slut and is graded 7.
Lower-off anchor.
The bolt at the start looks rusty and is unnecessary. Instead, traverse in from the right.
Fine crack climbing, but often wet.
The cracks furthest left on the steep wall.
Starts at the left edge of the steep wall. Corner.

Between Frank Olssons stuga and Skalmansväggen are two corners. Accésskommitén is the left-hand one. Fine corner climbing for about 20 metres, then up onto a ledge and from there following the next route to the top.
Corner to a ledge, then cracks to the top.
