Brötberget is one of Dalarna’s very best crags for lower-grade top-roping.

body.text: Brötberget is one of the very best crags in Dalarna for top-roping at the lower grades.

Climbing at Brötberget began in the late 1970s, when a group of climbers from Falun, led by Johan Luhr, found the crag. The rock is granite and, with a couple of exceptions, unfortunately offers little protection, so most routes are climbed on top rope. There are plenty of trees above the crag, although they can be some distance from the edge, so it is worth bringing a long static rope or long slings. Those wishing to lead will manage with a set of nuts and a few small cams up to Camalot #0.75. The routes are between 8 and 12 metres long, and the crag is approximately 200 metres wide, divided into two walls by a large gully.

From Falun: Follow road 266 towards Hedemora for 25 km. Shortly after passing beneath a large power line, turn right towards Vika Strand. Immediately after turning at the junction, a short side road leads to parking in a small wooded copse. Walk back across road 266; the crag is clearly visible on the other side of the field. Follow the edge of the ditch that crosses the field (do not walk across the field itself!) and then follow the edge of the woods 20 metres to the left to a rather hard-to-spot path leading up to the crag.

There have never been any records of who climbed what, or of route names, but most lines have been climbed either on top rope, using whatever protection was available, or solo. The routes below are a selection of the most obvious and best lines, and there is much more to discover. As only two of the routes have known names, the crag is described in continuous text.

Left-hand wall.

The easiest way to reach the top of the left-hand wall is to climb a short, steep gully (grade 2) at the far left. If climbing the gully without a rope does not feel comfortable, continue a little farther left and go round to the right, then descend onto the large ledge above the wall, at the far right when viewed from above.

The first thing you see as the path reaches the crag is a steep slab at the right-hand end of the left-hand wall. A striking crack system (4+) runs diagonally rightwards along the left edge of the slab. It can either be followed all the way or finished directly up the slightly steeper wall; it is very well protected. The slab also gives good climbing from the small “hollow” straight up (5+).

Immediately to the left are two obvious routes that have been brushed free of moss. The first is “Vid sidan av vägen” (6-), climbed by Ted Ekberg in 2005. It is better protected than it looks, though still far from well protected, but is good on top rope. (Definitely not climbed either before or after the FA except on top rope...)

The next route is the only bolted one, with two expansion bolts and a V-bolt that is not visible from the ground. Climbing straight up through the top-out is estimated to be 7+/8-, but by stepping onto some small edges and ledges a metre to the right, then traversing back left to make a spectacular splits move before climbing the top-out, the grade comes down to 6+ (FA Mattias Flodin, 1980s). There is an expansion bolt and a small pine above the edge for setting up a top rope.

Another couple of metres along, a spruce grows right beside the crag. Two good variants start at a section of black rock. Either climb straight up (5), or traverse left beneath a flake about three metres up to “Den siste alpinisten” (5, T. Ekberg, 2003), which follows a series of slightly hollow flakes. (This route may have been led before, but probably not, as it was dug out from beneath half a metre of soil and moss.) Both variants are reasonably protected with nuts and a medium-sized cam, although the first three metres are unprotected.

Right-hand wall.

The best way to reach the top of the right-hand wall is via the obvious gully/slope at its left-hand end. There are several easier top-rope routes on the side wall of the gully. It is also perfectly possible to walk round to the right, although this is a little more overgrown.

Continuing right past the large gully brings you to a slightly taller, mossy wall. At its right-hand end is a clean, attractive pillar offering several variants in the grade 3–4 range.

Farther right, the wall becomes more broken and mossy for a while before reaching an obvious pillar with a corner along its left edge. The corner (5) is quite good, but the lower section is poorly protected; the upper section is very well protected.

The steep wall to the right of the pillar offers the best climbing, but is considerably harder, with variants from 7- to 8-.

The fine crack system at the far right, starting above a small chimney (5+/6-), is one of the crag’s real gems and is very well protected if you wish to lead it.

Good tent pitches can be found on moss-covered slabs above the left-hand wall. They are clearly visible on the left as you walk up the large gully between the right- and left-hand walls. There is a fire ring below the left-hand wall. No water is available.

Follow the parking directions. Climbers previously used to park in the small pull-in beside the field, preventing the farmer from getting through with his tractor. Use the fire ring and take all rubbish home! Dalarnas klätterklubb has also expressed a wish to keep the crag free of bolts.

The crags are in Runby, Upplands Väsby. By car: Leave the E4 when approaching from Stockholm, heading towards Upplands Väsby C, and turn left at the roundabout onto Mälarvägen. Turn right at the roundabout just after Hemköp. Turn left onto Törnbyvägen. Park at the end of Törnbyvägen. Straight ahead, in the direction the road continues, you will see a small path; take it and pass a wooden barrier. Take the first path on the right and follow the edge of the meadow/tree line for about 100m. The crags are on your right.

A very fine crag 15 km north of Arvika. The rather generic name is pronounced in the Värmland dialect, with emphasis on the b, a short y and a long n, so you don’t sound like a lost Stockholmer.

body.text: A very fine crag 15 km north of Arvika. The rather generic name is pronounced in the Värmland dialect, with emphasis on the b, a short y and a long n, so that you do not sound like a lost Stockholmer.

From Arvika: Take Route 61 towards Karlsad. Turn left towards Gunnarskog.

From Karlstad: Turn right towards Gunnarskog just before entering Arvika.

From here it is about twelve kilometres to the parking.

Just past Lake Racken, a few crags can be glimpsed at the edge of the forest. One is Öjerudsklippan, which has good routes in the grade 6–7 range; the other is rubbish. Carry on. Soon a lake appears on the left. It is called Gunnern, and now it is time to start looking out. After a gentle left-hand bend followed by a gentle right, a small gravel road runs uphill to the right. It is marked by a sign featuring a dachshund, and the top of the crag is visible if you know where to look. Drive carefully up the gravel road and, after a left-hand bend, park equally carefully beside a small field.

Follow a small tractor track along the edge of the field into the forest. After a while, a faint path leads off to the right towards the crag. Five minutes at most.

The routes are closely grouped on a 50-metre-wide, slightly overhanging granite wall and offer fine, often fairly sustained climbing from start to finish. During the first visits in the early 1990s, the wall was unanimously deemed unclimbable. A few years later, it became clear that this was the best sport climbing crag in our part of the country, although the trad routes are only marginally less sporty. Slightly overhanging climbing on rounded edges and cracks. The rock quality ranges from good to very good, and friction is generally good. The crag gets the sun during the afternoon and evening.

All projects should probably be regarded as open by now, but do not place bolts without first contacting someone from Jösse Klättersällskap. See contact details at http://www.josseklatter.se.

Access

• Park so that you do not obstruct access.
• Show consideration for flora and fauna.
• Do not make excessive noise at or on the crag. Sound carries a long way once you are above the trees.
• Leave the place as you would wish to find it: clean and tidy.

The topo was created in the simplest possible way, using a poor base image with the lines drawn on afterwards. It may be of some help when looking for the lines at the crag. Click the images for larger versions. Solid pink lines indicate completed routes; dashed lines indicate projects. Yellow squares indicate bolts or anchors. All lines and bolt positions are approximate. Use the descriptions, images and common sense when identifying the lines. The number of bolts makes it fairly easy to work out where you are. All routes follow reasonably obvious lines.

Listed from left to right.

description.text: Starts in a small corner. A bit of scrappy climbing put up by Anders Borg/Tväråna during a cleaning day. Recleaned in 2009.

Shares the start of Gbs-ab, then heads directly left. Poor protection and classic Byn-style climbing make this a rather intimidating project. Recleaned in 2009.

Adequately protected. A perfect warm-up. According to several climbers, one of Arvika’s finest at the grade. What the abbreviation stands for has never been revealed, but it can be assumed to conceal some kind of insult aimed at a certain AB.
Well protected and a good warm-up. Starts a metre or so right of Gbs-ab.
Now fully bolted, 2009. A tricky lower half followed by an easier finish.
Fantastic. Gets harder the higher you go. An additional bolt was added at the crux in 2008, which should mean shorter falls and more ascents. Lower-off.
Shares the start of Odysséen to the no-hands rest in the hole, then goes left and straight up. Four independent bolts plus a lower-off. Fine climbing.

Superb climbing. 6+ according to Christian. Lower-off. An extra bolt has since been added, along with a new bolt allowing a more direct line over the shield, 2013. Note: the route goes more directly upwards than shown in the picture.
Follows the obvious crack system until it ends and the crux begins. Finishes on the ledge and shares an anchor with Kraft.
Follow the bolts to the ledge. Use the anchor on the right to access Konstant dyspné. Stays dry in the rain and makes a good warm-up.
The straight-up extension to Kraft from the anchor. A very fine climb.
Alternative extension. Follow the bolts to the ledge, clip a long sling into the Konstant dyspné anchor, then continue traversing to and up the finish of Debaeredret. A taste of Spain!
The crack leading to the anchor on the ledge. It is possible to continue to the top. Click the link for video beta.
Pump-inducing, with a lower-off. A notch harder than Odysséen!

Eight bolts plus an anchor. Pump-inducing and fine. Note: there is a risk of trapping the rope and/or breaking off the final hold/flake. Extend the lower-off and/or abseil rather than lower off. New lower-off added in 2012.
On the steepest part of the crag, with widely spaced nut placements. Unrepeated as of 2009.
If it is to be bolted, place the bolts so they do not interfere with Epik!
On red rock. A perfect warm-up if you know the moves. Lower-off.
Through the roof right of Brand. A balancey crux above the face. Anchor in a large pine tree.
Look for a bolt on the pillar immediately right of the roof. Go straight up the pillar.
Slab first, then overhanging. A new lower-off and an extra bolt at the start, added in 2013, should allow many more climbers to enjoy this gem.
A safely bolted warm-up with enjoyable moves. Four bolts plus a lower-off.
Finishes ten metres up in the pine tree.

An excellent crag for learning to lead trad. The crag faces south-west over an open field, providing plenty of evening sun. There are good opportunities for top-roping, but bring suitably long slings and/or a few nuts, as the trees are some distance from the top of the crag. The crag is 20 m long and varies between 3 and 8 metres in height, so with a crash pad and a healthy dose of cojones, you can leave the rope at home. Uppsala’s best after-work crag?

Take Dag Hammarskjölds väg south from the city centre and turn right onto Vårdsätravägen at the traffic lights by Ulleråker. Continue for a few kilometres to a turning signposted Hammarskog and take it across the bridge (Lurbo bro). Continue to the turning signposted Uppsala Näs on the left. Follow this road for 1 km, then turn right onto the gravel road that provides the back route to Hammarskog. After a couple of kilometres, you will see the Dalkarlskärret car park on the left. Cross the road and walk north-west around the lake for 10 minutes. You will see the crag on the right.

Alternative: instead of turning left towards Uppsala Näs, turn right at that junction, then turn left onto a gravel road. Pass a few houses and continue into the woods. You will soon find a parking bay on the right—park there or at the turning area a little further on. Follow the path opposite the first parking bay for 5–10 minutes (there are red markers). The crag is on the left at the edge of the field.

Another guide with slightly clearer route descriptions can be found at www.bergsklattring.nu. Link to guide (http://www.bergsklattring.nu/index.php?pg=/showpage.php&t=142)

The use of private roads is now strictly prohibited. The landowners have contacted the Finnish Climbing Association about the matter. If you are heading to Djupviken (the caves) or Fågelberget, park at Soltuna (café, holiday village, etc.) and walk from there.

Parking at Kasviken is very limited. If the parking by the field is full, find somewhere else to park or go elsewhere. Do not block access.

Take all rubbish home with you, including bits of tape and cigarette butts. Set a good example and pick up any other rubbish you find as well. The bin at the caves is emptied extremely rarely, so it is better to take your rubbish home than put it there. Last summer it was not emptied between May and October and was therefore overflowing for most of the year.

Åland (Finland) does not have the same right of public access as Sweden, so camping should be kept to an absolute minimum. Soltuna offers camping with facilities. Brush off tick marks.

For more information: http://www.slouppi.net/contents/news/moreNews.phtml?typeId=2 http://www.slouppi.net/pdf_files/Accesstiedote.pdf (in Finnish)

Härlanda Tjärn is a lovely swimming lake. Not far from the bathing area is Brudaremossen, a fairly broad crag, 10–12 metres high. Alternatively, follow the figure-of-eight trail to the left before/at the car park to reach Härlanda Tjärn/Västra långvattnet. There is also bouldering in the woods; see gbo-crimp.

body.text: Härlanda Tjärn is a lovely swimming lake. Not far from the bathing area is Brudaremossen, a fairly wide crag, 10–12 metres high. Alternatively, follow the figure-of-eight trail to the left just before/by the car park to reach Härlanda Tjärn/Västra Långvattnet. There is also bouldering in the woods; see gbo-crimp.
The area is a nature reserve and is extremely popular. Follow the instructions on signs at the car parks and throughout the area.

Take bus 17 to Spåntorget, then turn right and follow signs for the Härlanda Tjärn bathing area. The figure-of-eight trail starts just before the car park; the approaches to the various crags are described from here.
Approach: follow the figure-of-eight trail until you see the crags on the left, just under a kilometre.

From the car park at Härlanda Tjärn, follow the figure-of-eight trail, which starts just before the car park. When you reach a lake called Svarttjärn, keep right and follow signs towards Brudaremossen; the crag is on the left-hand side of the path. The crag is shady and dirty, and there are lots of mosquitoes here. However, it is fairly wide and has a great many unclimbed cracks. New-route potential!
Continue past Brudaremossen.

The area is on Ingarö, about 20 km east of Stockholm. Don’t expect bouldering on a sandy beach with the sea behind you, though. That might have been the case if you had come here a little earlier – a few thousand years ago or so.

Instead, the area lies deep in the forest. Despite this, it is not particularly dark or shady, as the trees are fairly sparse in front of the crags.

Development began in the summer of 1997, mainly by DD, Stefan Pettersson, Michael Meyer and Tomas Gustavsson. It is by no means fully developed.

The area is dominated by a relatively low rock face, an ancient fault scarp. The boulder problems are found both on this face, on several tiers, and on a number of boulders. The rock is granite. Much of it has been polished by ice and water, giving it its distinctive character. The landings are generally excellent, though there are exceptions. It is worth bringing a wire brush, as some problems tend to become mossy extremely quickly.

One thing to bear in mind is that many of the problems lie in the shade in the forest and are often wet.

body.text:
The area is on Ingarö, about 20 km east of Stockholm. Don’t assume this means bouldering on a sandy beach with the sea as a backdrop. That would probably have been true if you had come here a little earlier...a couple of thousand years ago or so.

Instead, the area lies deep in the forest. Despite this, it is not particularly dark or shady, as the trees are fairly sparse in front of the crags.

Development of the area began in the summer of 1997, primarily by DD, Stefan Pettersson, Michael Meyer and Tomas Gustavsson. It is by no means fully developed.

The area is dominated by a relatively low crag, an ancient fault scarp. The boulder problems are found both on this scarp, across several tiers, and on a number of boulders. The rock is granite. Much of it has been polished by ice and water, giving it its distinctive character. The landings are generally excellent, although there are exceptions. It is worth bringing a wire brush, as some problems tend to become extremely mossy very quickly.

Bear in mind that many of the problems lie in the shade of the forest and are often wet.

Take road 222 towards Gustavsberg. After about 20 km from Slussen, turn off towards Ingarö. Follow Ingarövägen through Brunn. About 4 km after Brunn, turn left towards Eknäs. After another 4 km, turn left towards Östernäs at the Skälsmara bus stop. Then turn right onto Brudbergsvägen and follow it to the end; when you see the turning area, you have arrived.

NB: Do not park in the turning area! Continue another 20 m into the forest and park on the left. Do not park on the forest road to the right either.

Take the main path to the left alongside the red summer cottage. Follow the power lines for a couple of hundred metres and the boulders will appear on the left.

Bus: Take bus 430 from Slussen to Skälsmara (50–55 min). Walk about 1.3 km (around 30 min to Sector 1).

Posted: Sat Aug 05, 2006 21:44

Hello, climbing sisters and brothers.

I was at Brudberget today (despite the heat? as John said when I bumped into him later, but I was in the shade). Anyway, the next person to join our session said that he had been told off by “the man in the red cottage” right by the turning area. The reason was that he had parked there, as I had, in the turning area—which looks incredibly like a car park—just as I have always done over the years. Anyway, the man in the red cottage wants us to continue along the forest road and park just beyond the turning area. Apparently, once he had said his piece, he became really friendly.

Well, this may be old news to everyone except me, but I thought I would share my revelation. The problem with parking on the forest ground is that everyone has to make a bit of an effort to fit in three, or at most four, cars. So drive well in and make sure people can get in and out.

I reckon that’s my access good deed done for the day.

Happy climbing! Jenny

Balancy and delicate; not exactly a power problem. On the back of the boulder. Start with your left hand round the edge where "Boulder für alle" finishes, and your right hand high in a sloping undercling. Step up and traverse all the way to the jug at the far end of the boulder. The top edge is out.

Balancy and delicate; not exactly a power problem. The reverse of "Brown fat...". Start on its finishing jug by the slab edge of the boulder and finish with a hand round the edge where "Borwn fat..." starts. The top edge is out.
One for basketball players? The two crimp edges are just over 3 metres up. Finish by mantelling over the edge.
A fine problem with a good landing. Starts with both hands on the good edge in the middle of the wall and finishes as Tutsi warrior.
A clean, beautiful problem with a good landing. Starts below the edge and joins Tromb at its starting edge.
A hard catch from a sit start, at the far right of the "Tutsi wall".
A couple of hard moves. Uses the same starting holds as "Core".

A fun traverse on nice holds, harder than it looks. Start with both hands in the corner, one on either side, and traverse the long slab. Finish on the good hold some way up at the far end of the wall.
A fun traverse on nice holds, harder than it looks. The reverse of "Ze balance". Start on its finishing jug, high on the edge, and finish with both hands on the starting holds of "Ze balance".
A fun but short catch. Sit start to Ze balance.
Follow the crack from the very bottom all the way to the top edge.
The direct line of The Queen Mother.
Sit start opposite the start of Slap...
A traverse with a perfect landing. Start at the far left with your hands on the holds above the small "triangle" and finish on the hold at the end of the horizontal crack.
A traverse with a perfect landing. Sit start, the same as "Sittstart Almost...", then reverse "Recreaton for the masses" and finish with both hands in its starting position.
A traverse with a perfect landing. Start hanging from the jam in the crack on the right, then reverse "Recreation for the masses" all the way to its starting holds.

High, intimidating and thin for the feet. Climb the crack all the way to the top. The crux is at the end... The tree may be used to save yourself and get down. Can also be done with a sit start, the same as "Recreation for the middleclass", at Fb6b.
A fine arete. Requires a good spot. Reach up to holds on the arete and climb to the top.

A super-cool problem on reasonable holds. Take plenty of pads. Starts deep in where the roof begins, from the same sit start as "Recreation for the middleclass", and finishes up the arete via "Aussie boys". "You can't find a problem better than this one in Fontainbleau"
An extremely low, strange problem. Much harder if you are tall.
Sit start with both hands in the small recess.
A long catch with a horrendous landing. Can only be done with a top-rope as insurance.
Starts behind the tree. Make an easy traverse right, then head up and top out. A little scary.
Exactly what the name says, with an awkward top-out. Take care.
Technical, strenuous and excellent. Sit start from the ground. The problem can be solved in several ways depending on your height.
Technical and very strenuous. Starts with both hands on the small ledge to the right of "And god...".
A pure, hard problem across the blank wall to the right of "And god...". Standing start from two sidepulls, one on the left and one on the right.
A footwork-sensitive traverse along the large crack high up between "Rastafari" and "When god pulled the trigger". Use a spotter as it is some way off the ground.
Easy but fun. The obvious large crack by the spruce, about 3–4 metres to the right of "When god pulled the trigger". Sit start.
A fun problem with a good landing. Start with both hands in the upper horizontal crack and both feet in the lower one. Double dyno.
Variant of "Flygande svensken".
Variant of "Flygande svensken".

A fairly balancey crack-jamming problem. Starts on the good holds down in the crack to the right of Flygande, then follows the thin crack upwards. Finish with both hands at the top of the sloping edge. Only the wall may be used, not the protruding section on the right.
A fairly hard sit start followed by easier climbing. Goes up the middle of the wall and finishes in the scoop at the top.
Unusually fun for V0 and not entirely obvious. Climbs the wall immediately beside the sloping edge on the right and finishes in the scoop at the top.
A short but fairly interesting problem with a good landing. Start with each hand on a crimper on either side of the recess.
Sit start with your left hand on the good edge used by the right hand on "Train in vain". Finish on the jug on the edge to the left.
The same line as "Skalman", but with a sit start and your left hand on the micro-edge beneath the good edge on "Skalman". The edge on "Skalman" is not allowed.
Starts on the same holds as "Skalman". A sit start is possible but not required.
Start with both hands on the two slopers.
Start with both hands on the two slopers.
A traverse starting just left of "Train in vain" and finishing just right of "Hatt" and "Mössa". Follow the crack; no eliminates.
A traverse on the boulder in the woods. Easy for the hands along the edge, harder for the feet.
An obvious line following an undercling crack rightwards.
Climbs the arete to the left of "Undercling thing". Start with your left hand in the undercling and your right hand on the pinch, with any footholds allowed.
The obvious line following a large crack straight up, to the right of "Undercling thing".
An almost perfect boulder problem with a perfect landing. Start with both hands low in the horizontal crack.
Another superb boulder problem with a superb landing. Start with both hands in the horizontal crack about a metre to the right of "Dharma". Shares the finish of "Dharma".
A very distinctive problem on excellent solid rock, with a good landing.
A superb long dyno on good holds.
Quite technical, but not particularly hard.
A fine, committing problem. It feels harder than it is.
The arete/wall immediately left of the Sandbag crack.
The wall left of the crack above Airbag, with a semi-dynamic finish.
A superb problem featuring crimps and a dyno.
The obvious crack.

Dynestad offers vertical and overhanging climbing on walls up to 18 m high. A very pleasant crag in a beautiful setting, which we highly recommend. The climbing is on solid granite, sometimes split by cracks. Stone was quarried at Dynestad in the late 19th century. Several of the routes are of exceptionally high quality. This is one of the best sport climbing crags we have. There are challenges at a range of grades for all climbers. The wall faces south and gets the sun from around 10.00. It dries relatively quickly after rain and is a good spring crag. The crag has 12 particularly recommended routes.

The climbing is on solid granite, though it is sometimes fractured. Stone was quarried at Dynestad in the late 19th century. Several of the routes are of very high quality. This is one of our best sport climbing crags. There are challenges at a range of grades for all climbers. The wall faces south and gets the sun from around 10.00. It dries relatively quickly after rain. A good spring crag. The crag has 12 particularly recommended routes.

The crag lies about 25 km north of Västervik. Leave the E22 at the northern exit towards Gamleby centre and continue for 1 km. Turn left at the square, drive past the folk high school and stop after 1 km. Immediately after a small bridge, turn right into the car park. Then follow the path south for 700 m, with Dynestadsviken on your right. The crag is visible on the left.

KustCamp campsite at Hammarsbadet is 2 km from Gamleby centre, tel. 0493-10221, www.campa.se (http://www.campa.se)

Överums hostel, tel. 0493-30302, www.overum.nu (http://www.overum.nu/),

cabins and camping at Risebo fritid, tel. 0493-33046, www.risebofritid.se (http://www.risebofritid.se),

camping and cabins are available at Lysingsbadet in Västervik, tel. 0490-258000, www.lysingsbadet.se (http://www.lysingsbadet.se),

cabins are available at Tofvehults café och konferens, tel. 0490-26351, www.tofvehult.se (http://www.tofvehult.se), and at

Live in Nature at Kallersnäs gård, tel. 0768-513090, www.liveinnature.se (http://www.liveinnature.se)
The nearest grocery shop, ICA, is in Gamleby centre, about 2 km from the crag. The nearest petrol stations, Bilia and Preem, are in Gamleby.

The meeting place for climbers is the centre in Västervik, at Repslagargatan 5 by the old water tower. Free wireless internet is available, or you can borrow a computer. It is a place to meet, share experiences, read climbing magazines, watch climbing films and get up-to-date information about climbing and other activities in the municipality. It is also a place where you can climb indoors and get to know other climbers. Call 0490-37466 for more information.
A guide produced by Västervik Climbing can be found at www.vastervikclimbing.se (http://www.vastervikclimbing.se/klattercentret/pdfer/).
