Climbing at Bispbergs klack has been completely prohibited since 1996, but it would be a shame if the existing documentation of the routes established there between around 1967 and 1987 – the period when virtually all of the crag’s more than 150 routes were put up – survived only in the memories of those who established them. Climbing at Bispberg is part of Swedish climbing history and deserves to be documented. NOTE: Topos will be added to this article as soon as they are ready.

body.text: Climbing on Bispbergs klack has been completely prohibited since 1996, but it would be a shame if the existing documentation of the routes established there between about 1967 and 1987—the period when virtually all of the crag’s more than 150 routes were put up—survived only in the memories of those who established them. Climbing at Bispberg is part of Swedish climbing history and should be documented. NOTE: Topos will be added to this article as soon as they are ready.

Take the train to Säter, from where it is about a 3 km walk along the road to the crag. By car: see map. For the final section by car, turn onto Carusovägen, take the first left immediately after the entrance to Klacken, park at the end of the road and then follow the waymarkers. The walk from the car park to the crag takes about 15 minutes.

Climbing on Bispbergs klack has been completely prohibited since 1996, but it would be a shame if the existing documentation of the routes established there between about 1967 and 1987—the period when virtually all of the crag’s more than 130 routes were put up—survived only in the memories of those who established them. In the memories of those fortunate enough to climb at Bispberg during the crag’s heyday, and in the few guidebooks that were published and now gather dust on the shelves of those who own a copy. Climbing at Bispberg is part of Swedish climbing history and should be documented. What better place to gather such documentation than Sverigeföraren? One of the submissions made to the County Administrative Board in connection with the creation of the nature reserve compared the significance of Bispbergs klack to Swedish climbing with that of Lake Mälaren and the Stockholm archipelago to Swedish long-distance ice skating. That comparison may be somewhat exaggerated, but it is not entirely without foundation. This compilation is based on Uppsala Klätterklubb’s 1982 Bispberg guide, the guidebook ”Klättring i Dalarna”, written by Johan Luhr, Anders Wester, Arne Johnsen and Lars Svadängs and published by Dalarnas Klätterklubb in 1986, and a supplement published by DKK the following year. Some corrections and additions of my own have also been made. The account in the history section of the creation of the nature reserve and the climbing ban was, however, written by the person who handled the matter on behalf of Svenska Klätterförbundet. As all the documents relating to the case are hidden somewhere in the Federation’s archives, this account is based on memory and is therefore both subjective and undoubtedly contains some errors of detail.

A complete year-round climbing ban has been in force since 1996. The decision can be read here: http://www.lansstyrelsen.se/dalarna/SiteCollectionDocuments/Sv/djur-och-natur/skyddad-natur/Naturreservat/Bispbergsklack/beslut-skotsel.pdf

With about 160 routes, many of which would hold their own on any Swedish crag, Bispberg deserves a better reputation than ‘that mossy crag in Dalarna’. The crag’s wooded setting creates a pleasant atmosphere for camping, but also makes it difficult to get an overview of the walls. This guidebook was produced to give visiting climbers a chance to try the best routes instead of wandering among the trees searching with glazed expressions. We also want to give a reasonably complete picture of what has been climbed at Bispberg so far, thereby supplementing the earlier guides published by us and Uppsala Klätterklubb. Many thanks to Uppsala Klätterklubb for providing information.

The steep part of Bispberg consists of several walls with a combined length of about 300 metres. At its highest, the crag is 80 metres. The rock is granite and is generally of very good quality. Nut protection is generally excellent, though pitons are encountered on most older routes. Some belays also have cemented-in bolts. With a couple of exceptions, the routes described have been carefully cleared of lichen, moss and other vegetation deemed troublesome by sensitive climbers. As well as the fine climbing, Bispberg’s pleasant atmosphere is enhanced by beautiful surroundings and excellent camping below the crag. Life for campers has been made easier by the exemplary efforts of handy locals from Dalarna, and there are now fireplaces (use only the existing ones), a wood store, a composting toilet and a water source conveniently close to the camping area.

The crag is divided into eight sections, with the routes described from left to right. Directions are always given as seen by an observer looking at the crag from below. Some rarely climbed or ‘trivial’ variants have been omitted, as have all routes that have only been climbed on a top rope. Opinions naturally differ on what constitutes a ‘good route’, but we have nevertheless attempted to rate their quality, which may help hurried visitors choose routes. Routes are rated using a star system, assessing the quality of the line and the climbing, as well as its consistency. A route with uneven climbing therefore receives few stars even if it contains highly interesting cruxes. Variants and very short routes have not been included in the ratings. The absence of stars does not necessarily mean that a route is not worth doing, and this will hopefully be clear from the description.

The UIAA scale has been used: 1–7 for free climbing and A1–A4 for aid climbing. The grade applies to the hardest passage on the route. Variants are graded according to the hardest passage of their independent section; sections shared with the main route may be harder.

This usually becomes relevant sooner or later. The gully between Explorerväggen and C-väggen is the usual descent route, with some scrambling in places and a fixed steel cable. It is also possible to walk around either side of the crag. The obvious gully between A-väggen and B-väggen may look tempting, but is very loose.

Bispberg’s climbing history has not been traced back any further than the mid-1960s. No doubt the odd passing mountaineer had eyed up the walls from the main road before then, and perhaps even considered visiting one day. According to usually reliable sources, the first more hands-on encounter with the crag was made by a few climbers from Dalarna in 1965. Development began quietly, with a few visits each season to climb random sections as training for mountain routes. A group from Stockholm led by Hasse Hellström are also said to have been up for a recce around this time. Klassikern on C-väggen, climbed and cleaned by Tommy Sandberg and Lasse Ström, became the first continuous route up the crag. The route acquired its present winding line in 1968; previously, climbers had gone up the loose gully in the middle of the wall. Tommy, Lasse and P-G Bjurman were then able to take their time picking off the best lines over the following years. Blåkulla was climbed in 1969. A-traversen and Tallbarrsleden followed the next year. The pioneers named routes according to their position on the crag, using a letter and number. Only the name B2 has survived from that period, and route names are now shaped by the first ascensionists’ more or less exuberant imaginations. Dalarnas Klätterklubb was founded in 1974, and the first major climbing meet at Bispberg was also held that year. This brought the crag to the attention of climbers from elsewhere in the country. At the following year’s meet, the first route on Svarta Plattan was climbed when Kenta Lindström and Anders Karlsson from Stockholm did Broderskapet. Kenta also managed to ‘check out’ Friheten on a top-rope. The Climbing Federation’s AGM was held at Bispberg in 1976, and during that meet Friheten began to emerge as ‘the route’ of the crag. Some of its aura may have faded since then, but Friheten is undoubtedly still very well worth doing. In autumn 1977, the impressive roof on the first pitch of Fru Lundgren—then called Fröken Lundgren—was climbed after resisting a series of more or less serious attempts. After Fru Lundgren, the search for new lines gathered pace. Jämlikheten on Svarta Plattan was the next victim. A bent wire marked the high point of a heroic attempt that had ended about ten metres up in the moss a couple of years earlier. Progress beyond ‘Alve’s nut’ looked utterly impossible until determined brushing and inspection on a top-rope revealed plenty of usable features beneath the moss. Armed to the teeth with wire nuts, Erik Zeidlitz finally dispatched the elegant and sustained first pitch of Jämlikheten. En krum historia, found, brushed and climbed in 1978, was perhaps the best discovery of that year. The section of wall to the right of Explorer had previously been widely dismissed as uninteresting, but no one had bothered to investigate the possibilities particularly closely. En krum historia was well hidden beneath lichen and moss, but with one pitch of genuine ‘pleasure climbing’ it is now one of Bispberg’s most popular routes. A number of harder routes were climbed the following year. During one intense weekend, visiting New Zealander Rick McGregor was sent up several lines that no one had dared attempt before. The results were Nya Z, the second pitch of Fru Lundgren, Corpsagan and En lång historia. Of the other routes climbed in 1979, Hökfallet, Ökad jämlikhet, Etiskheten and Sträckbänken are particularly worthy of mention. At the very end of the season, Svaplattan was ‘discovered’ far to the left of the crag. Frenetic brushing on a cold autumn Sunday laid the groundwork for several fine routes the following year, featuring a style of climbing new to Bispberg. Drill bolts were introduced on Svapron to protect free climbing, without too much anguished ethical soul-searching beforehand. Otherwise, 1980 was something of a quiet year for new routes, but the following year made it clear that the possibilities were far from exhausted. NSU and Röda nejlikan, established in the spring, are two ‘typical Bispberg routes’, combining sustained wall and crack climbing along thin cracks that take small nuts at intervals. A couple of pitches of enjoyable grade 4 climbing were found just left of the first pitch of A-pelaren: Den nakna rapan and BSA. For once, the 1981 Bispberg meet enjoyed dry weather, and alert visitors managed to snatch a few lines. Through their combined efforts, Lasse Grankvist and Bogi Palfay succeeded in free climbing the desperate Fångarnas kör on Svarta Plattan. In 1982, attention turned to the lower A-väggen. Huggormen and Adjunktens sommarlov were established. The highlight of the season, however, was Jan Liliemark’s free ascent of Fransk sak. The following year began with the formation of an unusual feature. Running water froze into ice on C-väggen, and Kent Andersson and Per Calleberg claimed the first ascent of the rarely repeated Midvintersagan on ice that was at times only a few centimetres thick. During 1983, the Dalarna climbers spotted the potential and, led by Johan Luhr, brushed out a large number of new routes, including Terror, Marie Antoinette and A-diagonalen. More desperate routes followed in 1984: Döda din chef, Bli fri and Häng dom högt. In 1985, Johan Luhr managed to work his way up the upper section of Anarkisten, completing Bispberg’s first 7+ route. A good many other routes in the grade 7 range were also established, including several on the overhanging wall above the first pitch of Mordförsöket, and there are now, in 1986, no fewer than 13 grade 7 routes. The Dalarna climbers opened up a new wall and named it Paradisväggen, which to date has eight routes. Some of them are well worth doing on your next visit to Bispberg. Finally: when will grade 8 come to Bispberg? Could it perhaps be in 1986? That question concluded the history section of the 1986 guide. With hindsight, we can confirm that the answer was yes. During 1986, Johan Luhr established three new grade 8 routes: Det hetsade barnet on A-väggen, Dynamisk reträtt on B-väggen and Kung Tung on Korpväggen. The campsite below C-väggen was in a forest clearing leased by Dalarnas and Uppsala Klätterklubbar from the landowner at the time, who thereby received some compensation because regular camping prevented that particular plot from being used for forestry planting. Relations with the landowner had been good until then; for example, he was invited to dinner every year during the annual Bispberg meet in early September. This writer has clear memories of ‘the farmer’ eating pea soup and drinking punch with the climbers. For reasons we can only speculate about, the terms changed when the land changed hands, and the club was forced to move the campsite. The 1987 supplement says: ‘Unfortunately, we have been forced to move our campsite and parking area. Turn onto Carusovägen, take the first left immediately after the turn-off for Klacken, park at the end of the road and then follow the markers. The walk from the parking area to the crag takes about 15 minutes. The campsite is passed halfway along.’ Moving the campsite from privately owned land to council-owned land was the first sign of a series of changes that would eventually lead to a complete climbing ban. The very brief account given here of that process is highly subjective and includes some personal opinions. It contains no untruths, however, and in most cases it should be possible to distinguish fact from personal opinion and perception.

title.text: Nature reserve with a climbing ban

body.text: During the Swedish Climbing Federation’s AGM in Stugun in 1992, rumours began to circulate that the Dalarna County Administrative Board was investigating climbing at Bispberg, and the Board was contacted immediately. The Federation demanded to be made an official consultee in the investigation. This proved a wise move, as what had happened was that the County Administrative Board had begun investigating whether a nature reserve should be established at Bispbergs klack. Normally, the only parties entitled to comment on the matter were the landowners and leaseholders affected. Others affected by a decision to establish a nature reserve, particularly one restricting the right of public access—that is, the general public, all of us who enjoy that right—had no opportunity whatsoever to comment. There were two reasons why the matter came under investigation at all. Firstly, Bispbergs klack is a so-called southern-flora mountain, a place whose exposure to the sun allows plants to grow that are not normally found this far north, and had been designated an area of national interest by an inquiry more than ten years earlier. Secondly, one of the landowners at Klacken had notified the County Administrative Board of their intention to fell the forest below the crag. The investigation and creation of the reserve were the Board’s way of preventing clear-felling in an area of natural beauty. So far, so good. The climbers did not think a clear-felled area would have been attractive either. The problem for climbing was that it emerged in various ways that climbing was being identified as a major threat to the environment at the crag, particularly to certain vegetation. Proposals for a complete climbing ban therefore appeared early in the investigation. Climbers considered the arguments for a total ban very weak, since the only sensitive plants that could be identified were hazel, alder buckthorn and field forget-me-not, and these grow below the crag, not on the rock. If climbers posed a threat to these plants, it should have been easy to remove that threat by establishing approach and descent paths. If these plants—which admittedly do not grow much further north than this but are fairly common only a few hundred kilometres to the south—were so worthy of protection that no one should be allowed to move through the terrain at all, then surely a complete ban on access rather than a climbing ban was called for, the climbers argued. The opposing side probably also realised that the arguments were weak, as several other supposed experts joined the debate. Without being asked for their views by the County Administrative Board, one person at the Swedish Species Information Centre in Uppsala and another at the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF) in Stockholm wrote to the Board, expressing broadly the same opinions. The gist of the letters was that it should be obvious to everyone that climbing posed a major threat to the environment at Bispberg, both to plants and animals, especially on the type of south-facing walls found there. Interestingly, it turned out—or may even have been stated in the letters—that neither writer had visited Bispberg. The Swedish Climbing Federation regarded the submissions as appallingly lacking in seriousness and pointed this out to the County Administrative Board. In addition, every member of the Federation’s board withdrew their supporting membership of WWF. Suddenly it was not only the plants at the crag that were threatened by the presence of climbers. Perhaps someone had realised that climbing could not be a major problem when these plants were still there despite 30 years of regular climbing on the crag. So animals were now brought into the picture. The argument now was that climbing should be banned to avoid disturbing nesting birds of prey. It was pointed out that there were not many birds of prey on the crag. There ought to be, it was argued, and the conclusion was drawn that the climbers were to blame for their absence. Climbing should therefore be banned. The Swedish Climbing Federation pointed out that if bird protection was the aim, there was no reason to ban climbing all year round. Climbing in late summer and autumn, once the chicks had left the nest, should not be a problem for bird conservation. New arguments were introduced. The issue was no longer the vegetation below the crag; it was now assumed that rare species of moss and lichen ought to be present and were threatened by climbing. To investigate the matter, an independent consultancy, Naturcentrum in Gothenburg, was commissioned to carry out a survey. They were to count species at various locations on the crag, and Kasper Kotake of DKK assisted by abseiling the biologists down the rock. Unsurprisingly, they mostly found map lichen, rock tripe, grey-blue lichen and other thoroughly common species. However, some interesting discoveries were made: one or two rarer crustose lichens were found on some of the most frequently climbed routes. By removing the commoner, more vigorous lichen species, climbers had allowed these rarer species to establish themselves and survive. In a telephone conversation, the County Administrative Board’s investigator admitted that this was an interesting finding, but also said that it would in no way alter the decision to impose a climbing ban in the reserve. By this point, the access officers at the Swedish Climbing Federation working on the case had begun to suspect that there were reasons other than nature conservation behind the proposed climbing ban. Rumours had been circulating for some time that the real reason for the ban was a landowner with a personal dislike of climbers in general. This rumour has never been officially confirmed, since a climbing ban cannot be imposed simply because one or more people want one. Officially, it must be based on nature conservation or other more robust arguments. However, it felt like confirmation when, at a consultation meeting convened by Säter Municipality in 1995 and attended by the Swedish Climbing Federation, Dalarnas Klätterklubb, the municipality, the County Administrative Board and the landowners, one of the landowners said something along the lines of: ‘Make no mistake: if this becomes a nature reserve, we won’t even be allowed to process windblown timber in our own forest. And then you damn well won’t be allowed to climb there either!’ Local residents who had no objection to climbing or climbers also said that the same landowner was probably behind the above-mentioned submission from WWF, as well as a letter from the Dalarna Botanical Society, since it was known that there were family connections or other ties between the landowner and people in these organisations. Officially, however, a landowner cannot tell a County Administrative Board what decision to make. But if the Board wishes to establish a nature reserve, it can do so only by buying the land or if the landowner agrees to make the land available for the reserve. Perhaps a County Administrative Board may agree to certain landowner demands in order to establish a reserve despite not having the funds to buy the land. In any event, the climbing ban at Bispbergs klack came into force in spring 1996, and few people have climbed there since. This was a tragic loss not only for Swedish climbing but for all outdoor recreation. The case showed that major restrictions can be placed on the right of public access without objective, factual grounds. It also showed that such restrictions are imposed by officials who are not elected and therefore cannot be voted out at the next election. Otherwise, this would have given the public an opportunity to show its dissatisfaction with certain decisions because, as the case demonstrated, the public is not an official consultee. For climbing, it was a loss because Bispberg was the only crag in Sweden where beginners and intermediate climbers could gain solid experience of multi-pitch trad climbing—up to three pitches—on a single crag, in preparation for trips to bigger mountains abroad. It is still possible to gain such experience today, but not all in one place. The present writer spent two consecutive weeks at the crag in summer 1982, climbing almost every route up to grade 5+ as training for long routes abroad.
A new wall opened in 1985. Steep, sustained climbing, though unfortunately sometimes a little short. Easy climbing leads up to the starts of the routes.

A compact granite slab with just the right amount of texture, about 150 metres left of the old campsite, offering one pitch of climbing with a 45 m rope. Descend via an earthy ramp that comes down just right of the start of Svaheten. Advances in climbing shoes have made some friction routes easier than their original grades. These routes retain their original grades.

A 50-metre wall with few obvious lines. The wall is divided halfway up by a broad ledge and a short horizontal crack (A-traversen). To reach the upper routes starting from the ledge halfway up the wall, it is best to climb the first pitch of A-traversen or one of the routes on the lower section. At the very top is a smaller wall shaped like a Z.

Bounded on the A-väggen side by a loose gully and on the right by a distinct ‘nose’ (Frenetiska pelaren), where the face changes from south-facing to east-facing. B-väggen is about 80 metres high at its tallest point, and the angle is mostly quite amenable. Good rock, plentiful features and a short approach have contributed to a dense network of routes.
A broken wall between B-väggen and the usual descent route. Long ledges and several overhangs at the top mean there are no continuous routes up the entire wall.

The wall between the descent and C-väggen. Korpväggen is steep and loose in places. However, it has potential for new routes, which may appeal to some. The existing routes should not be forgotten, though, including Corpsagan and Påssjukan.

A long, high and broken wall. Loose rock in several places makes it suitable training for mountain climbing. To the right, the wall merges into a steep, partly vegetated slope. There are several small walls here with shorter sections of climbing. Useful landmarks for orientation are the loose gully crossed by Klassikern, the box at the second belay of Blåkulla, and CG's superlinje.

A smooth wall at the far right, with some of Bispberg’s best routes. Over the years, the slab has become less black as lichen and moss have been worn and brushed away. A diagonal ramp with several trees starts low on the left and divides the slab into upper and lower sections. Jämlikheten, Direkta jämlikheten, Broderskapet, Terror and Ryggskottet start partway up the ramp. These routes can be reached by: a) climbing the ramp from the bottom left (a section of 4+); b) climbing the ramp from the top right (a little difficult to find); c) traversing in from the right at the same height as the start of Broderskapet (mossy 3); or d) climbing one of the routes on the lower wall.

description.text: The corner left of Lilla arêten. One bolt near the start.
The short arete on the left. One bolt on the unusual finish.
The same start as Lilla arêten, then straight up the unprotected corner. Then move right onto the slab (crux).
The corner right of Ögon blå.
Short, intense laybacking up the obvious crack. Somewhat difficult to protect.
The arete on the left; delicate climbing protected by one bolt and small wires. NB: The crack to the left (Niklas blodiga kamp) must not be used.
The arete on the right. A brutally sharp start followed by enjoyable climbing. One bolt and small wires.
Start 5 m left of the main slab. The route follows a poorly protected shallow corner.
A well-bolted, weaving line left of Svapron.

Sustained, consistent friction climbing on perfect rock along the crest of the slab. Protected by three bolts. Variant: Fegisleden (4+). Traverse left at the first bolt and climb to the easy groove. Follow Svapron for the final section.
The crux is poorly protected.
Protected by three bolts. Start variant: Spansk peppar (6-).
The diagonal line on the right-hand side of the slab. The same finishing corner as Psycho sva. A suitable introduction to the mysteries of friction climbing, though somewhat difficult to protect.
The obvious short corner left of OB+. Not shown on the topo.

Not shown on the topo. Located left of A-väggen. Follow ramps and grassy ledges 20–30 m up the slope to a large upright flake visible from the old campsite. The route follows the chimney above the flake.

Usually climbed in four short pitches. Walk up vegetated ledges to the left of a tree split by a large boulder. The climbing begins up an obvious 8 m corner above a juniper bush. After the corner (two bolts at the crux), follow the ledge right across large blocks to a belay. Continue right for about 20 m to a belay at a tree. Climb the corner to another belay, then continue up the face. Varied climbing in fine positions. Variant: Utanför suggan (4). An airy traverse around the outside of the large blocks on the ledge instead of scrambling over them. Recommended.
Three short face sections, the first clearly the hardest, but alas unprotected.
Right of Snipp snapp snorum. A small, somewhat dirty corner.
The thin crack line left of Förspelet.
Start up an awkward corner behind a large block, Suggan. Finishes up a fine crack. Start variant (5): Climb A-brottet’s starting crack, but continue straight up to the crack of Förspelet.
Thin, unprotected face climbing.
The same start as the previous route. Undercling to a bolt above the roof, then straight up. Pumping.

Climb straight up behind the large blocks on the ledge to two parallel diagonal cracks. The crux is at the finish, which is slightly difficult to protect. Start variant (5): The corner just right of the original start, with an elegant finish.
The obvious corner and crack line right of A-brottet. Sustained, steep crack and face climbing. Well protected.
More or less straight up between Hökfallet and NSU. Two bolts near the start, followed by sustained face climbing. Finish variant (6+): Straight up past one bolt.

Exposed and sustained face climbing in the centre of the upper section of A-väggen. Poorly protected at the start. Follow the large ledge on A-traversen to two pitons placed side by side. Aim for the lower part of the diagonal crack in the middle of the face. Follow the crack until it is possible to climb straight up.
Start a few metres right of Hökfallet, aiming for a black angle piton. Then jam the crack. The same finish as Vinna eller försvinna.
Protected by a piton and small wires.

Starts a couple of metres left of the corner at the beginning of the third pitch of A-traversen. Climbs straight up the face. The finish (crux) can be avoided by taking the corner to the left instead (the finish of A-diagonalen). Protected by a bolt and a couple of pitons, but not much else.

The long diagonal line on the upper A-väggen. Climb the first 4 m of NSU, then follow the crack up and right. The finish is an easy corner a few metres right of the finish of Hökfallet. Fine, sustained climbing.
Thin, sustained face climbing. A ledge halfway up can provide a welcome rest. This makes the route less sustained, but no easier. Take Stopper #4 and smaller.
Climbs left of Huggormen, starting from the ledge above the uprooted tree. A well-protected crux.

Starts behind an uprooted tree, directly below Suggan. Begin in a corner and, after a few metres, swing right onto the face. Continue straight up. Sustained climbing with spaced protection. Start variant (6-): Begin up the start of Adjunkten, but traverse left beneath the roof to Huggormen.
Climb Huggormen to the upper horizontal crack, then hand-traverse along it to the original finish of Fransk sak.
The same start as Huggormen, then straight up over two overhangs. The face above the overhang is likely to cause the most difficulty. Four bolts and one piton. Plenty of climbing.
Starts left of Adjunktens sommarlov. Climb to a bolt beneath an overhang. Climb over it to two pitons, then straight up past another piton in the horizontal crack and one bolt. Thin face climbing.
A short, hard boulder problem. Start as for Lärarkandidaten, then move right past a bolt.
Start up a 5 m corner directly below the start of NSU. After the corner, climb (improbably) straight up. Sustained, difficult face climbing with some suspect peg protection.
Between Adjunktens sommarlov and Fransk sak. (On the first ascent, there were slings on the first and third bolts.) Thin face and friction climbing. Bispberg’s first route in the grade 8 range.
An old A2 route, now freed and given a direct finish. Delicate face climbing on perfect rock, but with poor rests. Bolts, pegs and a few wires.
Between Fransk sak and Kellogs. Three bolts. Thin edge climbing.
Runs 5 m right of Fransk sak. Thin face climbing at the start (bolt). Combined with Direkta Hökfallet, it is definitely worthy of two stars.
Starts by a large tree (which must not be used), just left of BSA. Leads straight up to the finishing crack of Den nakna rapan. The start is the crux.
Start left of a large hazel bush. Climb to a thin horizontal crack, traverse left and continue straight up to a ledge. A hand crack leads up to A-traversen, which can then be followed further.
Start as for Den nakna rapan. Instead of traversing left, continue straight up past several pegs. Fine face climbing.
Start as for A-pelaren, then continue straight up all the way to the top.

Start in the corner immediately left of the broad pillar on the right-hand side of A-väggen. The start is behind some dense bushes. The crux is swinging out onto the pillar after about 10 m (alternatively, climb diagonally up left). The final pitch is shared with A-traversen.
Short crack left of and below Z-väggen.
Traverse the whole of Z-väggen from left to right. Finish up Zuider Zee.
The traverse (crux) can be done with either hands or feet in the crack.
Short but steep hand-jam crack.
Finger crack leading to the finish of Z-leden; the rest speaks for itself.
Start right of S.P.B. and continue straight up to a bolt, then on past another bolt. Finish left of Nya Z. Thin face and friction climbing.
Sloping crack right of Nya Z.
Small wall a little further right. Two bolts.
Not shown on the topo. At the far left is a rarely climbed, moderately uninviting 10 m chimney.
The slab right of Kaminen. One bolt before the top.

About 20 m right of Kaminen. Same start as Tallbarrsleden. Climb a couple of metres to a vegetated ledge that leads left to the base of a 4 m chimney. Belay at a tree. The climbing proper starts here and follows the crack system beginning with the 4 m chimney.

An elegant passage through the large roof high on the left of B-väggen. Start as for Skivleden, but after the 4 m chimney traverse 3 m right to a corner. Climb towards the roof. A couple of metres below the lip, make a delicate traverse right to a thin crack and continue up over the roof.

(The route’s original name was B1.) A varied and interesting classic. Start 5 m left of a long corner line (Par i tak). Vertical features and rightward traverses (risk of rope drag) lead to a ledge with a pine tree (belay). Move to the right-hand edge of the ledge, climb up a little and traverse right to a groove. Follow this until a leftward traverse line leads out to a belay at a large pine tree. A fine crack with a wedged block leads to the top.

Start from the belay just below the first traverse on Tallbarrsleden. Make a rising traverse right across a prominent corner (Par i tak). Climb a few metres, then make a delicate traverse left (a little hard to protect) into a shallow chimney. Belay (small) below a square roof. Climb the roof and continue up, trending slightly right. Some loose blocks.

A long corner line interrupted by two distinct roofs. Start in the corner 5 m right of Tallbarrsleden and follow it all the way to the top (short traverse left at the first roof). Belay below the second, square roof. If you still have the energy after the first pitch, a combination of Etiskheten and Sendraget (through the roof) is a good alternative to the final pitch.

Starts from the belay below the square roof on Svarta september and Par i tak. Traverse left beneath an overhang. Then aim for the left-hand edge of the large roof crossed by Sendraget. Avoid the roof by slipping past its left-hand edge. Enjoyable climbing in fine positions.

Same start as Tallbarrsleden, but where Tallbarrsleden traverses right, climb diagonally left into a shallow corner (protected by small wires). Where the corner ends, climb diagonally up right (bolt) to the left-hand edge of an overhang. Traverse right across the overhang, with hands above it and feet below, until it is possible to climb straight up through the overhang (peg), then continue to the belay below the square roof.

The first pitch follows the corner to Fru Lundgren’s first belay. On the second pitch, traverse left to a peg, then continue over a roof to a corner and straight up past a bolt. The final metres are shared with Svarta september.
Starts left of Fru Lundgren. A short, awkward slab, followed by laybacking until it ends. Step out right. Considerably harder than it looks. Bring a no. 1 Friend.
Instead of stepping out right, continue straight up. One bolt.
Left of Fru Lundgren. Climb the overhang, then move left and out onto the slab. A little hard to protect.

In the middle of the wall is a 15–20 m-long roof system about 5 m above the ground. Fru Lundgren tackles the left-hand side of the roof with difficulty. Start on an outside edge left of a break in the roof (Hell's Gate). Climb up and traverse left beneath the roof (bolt). Pull through the roof and cross the slab to the first belay on Tallbarrsleden. The second pitch follows a corner system just left of the belay. The start of the second pitch is difficult and poorly protected unless you have a no. 3 Friend. After another overhang, you can catch your breath at the final belay on Tallbarrsleden.
Straight up over the overhang between Fru Lundgren and Jordemoderns klagan. Well protected. The easiest way through the overhang.
Same start as Fru Lundgren, but continue straight up the corner instead of traversing left. Continue straight up to the first belay on Tallbarrsleden.
Start at the first belay on Tallbarrsleden. Climb a thin crack just left of the pine. Climb the slab and pull over the next roof (long reach) to the final belay on Tallbarrsleden. Fine climbing.

Start in the left-hand corner of the long roof, right of Jordemoderns klagan. Pull over the roof (protected with a no. 2 Friend). Then continue straight up over the small roof at the start of the second pitch of Tallbarrsleden, and via a triangular roof to the second belay on Tallbarrsleden.
Right of Hell's Gate. Climb the overhang. Strenuous. One bolt.
Climbs through the roof right of Hell's Gate. Protected by a bolt.
The roof up by the Sjöfallslederna routes. Climb through the roof, then traverse left and continue straight up the short slab (bolt).
The overhanging corner leading straight up to the belay spruce on Mordförsöket. Friends are useful.
Start at the pine above Skräck. Swing out right, then follow the crack system left. Sustained and steep, but with good nut placements.
Right of Skräck. A short wall with two bolts. Harder than it looks.
Starts right of Stålnäven. Easy climbing up to the overhang.

Behind this charming name lies a route beginning up a 30 m ramp beneath overhangs, just right of the long roof system. Where the ramp ends (belay at a spruce), follow the large gully above until the route joins Tallbarrsleden. The first pitch (crux) is elegant and better protected than it looks. The second pitch is mossy and dangerously loose. Best combined with Tvärgatan and then other routes. Variant: Tvärgatan (3). A pleasant 20 m traverse between the belay spruce on Mordförsöket and the pine above the first roof on Fru Lundgren.
The overhanging wall above Mordförsöket. Bolt with a sling at the lip. Incredible.

Same start as Mordförsöket. At the crux of Mordförsöket, continue straight up with strenuous climbing. Bolt with a sling above the overhang. Continue to a tricky layback leading up to a small pine. Friends are useful.
Climb Trombostest to the second bolt, then move left and climb towards a bolt with a sling. Strenuous.

Start immediately left of the wall where B2 begins. Follow a pair of cracks past a spruce to the top of a huge pointed block. Place protection around the top of the block and gather your courage for the steep wall above. Continue via a short ledge and a small corner to a belay at a small pine, shared with Stora Sjöfallet and Direkten. Continue up Direkten, for example.
Traverse along the ‘lip’ above Mordförsöket to a small opposing wall, with a bolt below. Then continue straight up. Delicate climbing and not particularly well protected.

Probably the most frequently climbed route on the crag. Start a few metres left of the nose of B-väggen, behind two trees, in a short corner that eases onto a ledge. Follow the ledge upwards to a large pine (belay 10–15 m above the ground). An easier start leads diagonally in from the left to the pine. Second pitch: move a metre or so right and climb into the corner (crux, bolt). Move right and climb to another pine belay. Continue upwards, trending slightly left through alternating steeper and gentler sections. A fine introduction to Bispberg, particularly when combined with Lilla Sjöfallet.

From the second belay on B2, head diagonally up left. Step around the edge into a crack (bolts) and traverse to a pine (belay). Continue straight up the crack. From there, either continue straight up or traverse left and follow Tallbarrsleden to the top. Fun.

From the second belay on B2, climb down a couple of metres and traverse left, around an edge and across to a sloping ledge with a small pine and a bolt (belay, shared with Direkten). Continue left and climb the wall past bolts until the route joins Lilla Sjöfallet. Also fun. Variant (4): Go further left of Stora Sjöfallet and climb up to Lilla Sjöfallet.

Not the most logical line in the world, but fine climbing. Start in a crack beneath a large, obvious flake, ‘Skäran’, as for Direkten. With difficulty, step into the upper of the two banana-shaped cracks branching left from the starting crack. Continue to the first belay on B2. A couple of metres above the pine, make a slightly improbable traverse left to a crack hidden from the belay. Follow the crack for 8 m, then a small corner on the left (shared with Äckliga leden), to a sloping ledge with a small pine. The route ends here. Continue via Direkten or Stora Sjöfallet.
Starts at the first large pine on B2 and forms the second pitch of Fallrepet. Straight up the slab. One bolt.

This excellent route also goes by other poetic names, such as Direkta B2. Start as for Fallrepet in the crack about 10 m left of the ‘nose’, but follow the crack straight up. Climb the elegant flake/crack formation (Skäran) above the ledge. Continue into the crux corner of B2:2, then straight up a thin crack (easy to miss!). Follow the crack to its end, then step left by a pine, joining Stora Sjöfallet. Follow a corner, then cracks straight up to the top. Two long pitches of sustained, fine climbing.
Starts immediately right of Skäran on the first pitch of Direkten. It then climbs between B2 and Frenetiska pelaren (one pitch). Perhaps a little difficult to find.
A short route between Direkten and B2, high on the wall. One bolt.
Start left of Frenetiska pelaren. Friction and face climbing past a bolt to the first bolt on Frenetiska pelaren. Then follow Frenetiska pelaren upwards.

Delicate climbing along the ‘nose’. The route finishes at B2’s second belay. Protected by one and a half bolts. Variant (6): At the first bolt, continue straight up to a second bolt on the right, then straight up.
Start as for Frenetiska pelaren, but instead of going left, step out right at Frenetiska pelaren’s first bolt. There is another bolt higher up the route.

The route follows a line just right of the ‘nose’ throughout. Start in a corner a metre or so right of Frenetiska pelaren. At a pair of bolts, make a delicate step right, then traverse further right. Move back slightly left and climb cracks to a belay. It is easy to escape onto B2 from here. The final two pitches follow a series of ramps up and right. Belay before the final pitch at a dense spruce beneath an overhang. Three long pitches. Variant (4+): Climb the start of Pionjären, then after about 10 m follow the brushed line left.
A low-level traverse close to the ground between Pionjären and Explorer.

Start up a cracked wall with a couple of pines. Continue upwards until a system of ledges leads out right. It is easy to escape onto B2 from here if you have already had enough. Otherwise, follow the ledge system right for about 20 m. Climb a wall (crux) to another large ledge. Walk off right to the normal descent route.

A crescent-shaped crack system with a well-protected, technical and well-brushed jamming/bridging crux. Start left of and below En krum historia. Follow a corner for 6 m, then traverse left into the main crack system. A belay is soon reached out left. Climb the crux as a short pitch to a pine. The crack system ends after another short pitch. Continue right along Explorer or abseil.
The crack system between Sträckbänken and En krum historia. The short wall below the first belay is the crux. The second pitch follows an easy ramp up to Explorer, which is followed out right.
Follow Mutatis mutandis to the top of the corner, then move left and climb straight up past three bolts. Hard crux at the second bolt.
An elegant, sustained pitch (45 m rope) combining friction and crack climbing. Abseil from the belay (cemented-in bolt). It is also possible to climb up to Explorer.
Follow the corner to a bolt and up to the crux of En krum historia. Then follow En krum historia.
Fiendish slab climbing. Two bolts.
A gymnastic finger crack leading to the belay on En krum historia. Short, well-protected crux.

Climb down the back from the belay on En krum historia, then up to the right. Traverse right for about 10 m. The climber is now on a blank slab, a long way from home and a long way from the last piece of protection. Climb over bulges (crux, poorly protected) and a short wall to Explorer.
Climb up to a large, impressive right-facing corner about 10 m left of the cave. The crux is an awkward exit from the corner.
Right of Påssjukan, over a ‘bulge’. Powerful climbing, with two bolts.
The overhanging corner right of Kung Tung. Two bolts and one piton. Powerful.

Follows the steep corner 5 m right of the cave. The small ledge just below the roof is reached from the left. Somewhat loose and dirty up to the roof, followed by strenuous jamming and laybacking on sound rock. Belay on a large grassy ledge. Continue up through the woods.

Usually climbed in two short pitches, plus an escape pitch. Start as for Corpsagan. Follow the steep corner until a narrow ledge leads right by a juniper bush. Hand-traverse or balance along the ledge (exposed) and move out to a belay. Continue up and right. Belay on a large ledge. Scramble up through vegetation. Strenuous in places!
Left of Korpväggen, past one bolt. A gorilla route...
The overhanging brownish-yellow wall right of the first pitch of Korpleden. Two bolts.
Climbs a small corner between Korpväggen and Pappa sa rätt. Two bolts.
The steep arete 10 m right of Korpleden. Fine, strenuous climbing.
The obvious corner just right of Pappa sa rätt. Well protected and interesting. Not as easy as it looks.
The gully between Korpleden and CG's superlinje. Two pitches. Ice and mixed climbing on very thin ice. Only forms in some years. Repeated only once since the first ascent.
Starts at the foot of Midvintersagan. Climb an easy crack, then move onto the slab past a ring bolt and a bolt (crux). Higher up, the route crosses CG's superlinje and continues diagonally up right.
Located on a short, steep wall between Midvintersagan and CG's superlinje. Follow the crack in the centre of the wall. Strenuous.
Still uncleared when this edition went to press.

A faint path past a cairn leads to the start on a level ledge with a birch. Climb more or less straight up to a belay, which is not visible from the start. Climb an obvious ramp right, then up a few metres (possible belay). Continue up left and past a juniper bush to a large belay ledge, where there is a box containing a visitors’ book. The third pitch goes straight up the crack system at the left edge of the ledge (somewhat loose at the top). A long, classic Bispberg route. Variant (4-): From the first belay, climb straight up to the second, large belay ledge. Poorly cleaned.

A rather wandering line in the middle of C-väggen. Approach: Follow a faint path to a small cairn. Scramble up a 5 m gully to a sloping ledge with bolts. The climbing starts here, as for Klassikern. Traverse left for a few metres, then climb an awkward corner. Continue up for a few metres (bolts), then make an unlikely traverse right across a short slab. Aim for a narrow ledge with two small pine saplings. Follow the ledge, then descend slightly until a crack system leads straight up. Follow the crack system (belay after a few metres) until it becomes advisable to branch off right. Follow an easier, broken line (slightly loose) upwards. It is possible to traverse off to Blåkulla at several points along the route.
Start right of Luran. Follow Luran for a short way, past the small pine saplings, after which the route goes roughly straight up over short walls and ledges. It can be difficult to find.

Bispberg's first climbing route. Originally it largely followed the loose gully; the recorded first ascent refers to the current line. Uneven and rather wandering. Start from the same sloping ledge as Luran. Cross the green slab to the right, then continue traversing to an earthy gully with trees. Belay. Climb up until a slab leads out right. Climb the slab (belay), then continue well to the right until it becomes reasonably easy to climb up (Skräpleden joins here). Belay on a sloping ledge. Traverse left across large, unstable flakes (Sågklingan, exposed), then climb up. Direct variation (5+): Go straight up from the long traverse (arrow painted on the rock). Climb slightly right, then follow the final metres of Klassikern.

Fairly clean these days, but a little loose. Start where the wall drops down again after a broken, vegetated section. Start up a short, steep wall. From the ledge above, climb the crack system to a belay by a miserable pine. Follow the corners above to easier ground. Belay on a sloping ledge (shared with Klassikern). One short pitch straight up leads to the top.
Between Blåkulla (the ledge with the visitors' book) and Klassikern.

Starts to the right of the large hole in C-väggen, by the memorial plaque to Bruno Rabogliatti. Climb the sustained pillar, then traverse left and climb the next pillar. Poorly protected. Wonderful positions.
Scrambling with short climbing sections up the vegetated area forming the right-hand boundary of C-väggen.
Just left of Svarta plattan. A 20 m wet gully that usually freezes in winter. Slightly more sustained and steeper than the Oxberget icefall at Sifferbo.
Start from the diagonal ramp. Chimney behind a large flake up to a large roof. Awkward section with a razor-sharp jam/lock, followed by a short traverse right to Friheten.

Fine crack and flake climbing. Start from the diagonal ramp. After a 3 m smooth corner (4+), belay and start by a pine. Climb a crack system leading towards the roof, traverse left, then follow obvious crack systems upwards. Near the top, avoid a short, smooth wall (poor bolt) by detouring right before climbing into the hole on the left. Possible belay halfway up by a birch. Direct start (5+): After the smooth 3 m corner, traverse 2 m left to an enjoyable finger crack and follow it for 6 m.

Starts at the foot of Friheten but continues up through the overhang and then between Friheten and Fångarnas kör. Crux left of Fångarnas kör. The route then continues more or less straight up to the top. Belay from two bolts at the finish. Warning: several variations/dead ends have been cleaned; choose the correct line. The route is said to be equipped with five bolts.
A 15 m thin crack between the birch halfway up Friheten and Giljotinen. Very sustained. Then continue straight up past a bolt, or climb an easy ramp to the left.

Start by a pine on the large ramp, midway between Friheten and Jämlikheten. An unprotected wall leads to a ledge, followed by a fine crack. Traverse out to Friheten or climb straight up Fångarnas kör.
Runs just right of Schavotten, straight up to the crux of Giljotinen. Climb this too, then continue trending left. Exquisite climbing. Some pegs and bolts.
Direct finish to Döda din chef. Thin face climbing protected by two bolts. Firé (the first shoe with high-friction rubber, released in 1984) and long arms recommended.

Bispberg's finest route? Perhaps; at any rate, one of its most frequently climbed. Start from a belay by some bushes, a few metres left of Broderskapet. Climb left to a crack running straight up. Follow the crack to a horizontal crack (Giljotinen), then traverse right to a pine (belay). From there, climb straight up to the top.
The vertical crack forming part of Jämlikheten begins on the lower wall. Climb through roofs, then follow the crack all the way to the top. Very fine and sustained climbing.
Technical finger crack straight up to the belay pine on Jämlikheten. Approach from the left.

In the centre of the compact wall between Jämlikheten and Broderskapet. Start left of Styvbrodern, cross it and continue straight up to a peg driven into Giljotinen. From the peg on Giljotinen, superb face and friction climbing leads all the way up. Bolts. One of Bispberg's gems.
The obvious crack on the right-hand side of the upper wall offers fine and varied climbing.
Starting variation to Broderskapet. A diagonal crack joining Broderskapet from the left.
Traverse from Broderskapet to Friheten. Crux left of the belay pine halfway up Jämlikheten.
Diagonal crack line right of Broderskapet. Better climbing than it looks.
Short crack right of Ryggskottet.
Bispberg's rightmost crack.
Face climbing at the far left of the lower wall. Go left (counter-intuitively) at the stump a couple of metres up. Bolt.
Same start as Robespierre (and Röda nejlikan). Go straight up from the stump. Bolt below the roof; crux.

Thin crack directly below Friheten. The crack first runs diagonally right, then more or less straight up. Well protected, sustained and varied. Starting variation (6-): Skip the diagonal section of the crack and instead climb straight towards the vertical crack.
10 m right of Röda nejlikan. Easy climbing to a roof (bolt), then climb up, first trending right, then moving out left onto a ledge and from there straight up.

Starts from the same place as Marie Antoinette. Climb trending right to the overlap forming an inverted V, clip the bolt (set a little high) and pull up on the undercut. Continue upwards and clip the bolt on the wall above. Stepping up onto the wall is the crux. Continue over the short wall, then finish via Marie Antoinette.
The layback flake right of Marie Antoinette and Svarte Petter.
Climb over two overlaps, then trend right to a bolt. Continue straight over the overhang. Strenuous climbing.
Short crack by the large pine above Anarkisten.
Just left of Lillebror. A desperate start followed by difficult climbing. Finishes up a finger crack that is clearly visible from below. Strenuous.
The left-hand branch of a Y-shaped crack, below Broderskapet. Steep.
The right-hand branch of the Y-shaped crack. A roof halfway up can also be climbed direct.
A typical Gothenburg winter crag. Mid-grade sport climbing (roughly 6c–8a).

body.text: A typical Gothenburg winter crag. Mid-grade sport climbing (roughly 6c–8a).
